title: 2FA (Two Factor Authentication, TFA, Two Step Verification)
description: A two factor authentication is a process of confirming a user's alleged identity by using a combination of two different components, for instance a password and a physical token (e.g. PIN and credit card). Two-factor authentication (2FA) is method of access that requires two different forms of authentication.

title: 51% Attack (Fifty-One Percent Attack)
description: 51% attack is the name for a concerted attack on a distributed ledger system where miners controlling more than 50% of the system's hashing power can manipulate transactions. If more than half the computer power or mining hash rate on a network is run by a single person or a single group of people, then a 51% attack is in operation.

title: 0x Protocol
description: 0x is an Ethereum-based open-source platform for exchanging cryptocurrencies. It allows for the creation of features in a decentralized exchange (DEX), a wallet, or a marketplace.

title: 1hr
description: Stands for data for the past 1 hour.

title: 24hr
description: Stands for data for the past 24 hours.

title: 30d
description: Stands for data for the past 30 days.

title: 52-Week High/Low
description: A 52-week high and low is the highest and lowest market price of a given asset over 52 weeks or one year.

title: 52-Week Range
description: A 52-week range is a difference between an asset’s highest and lowest prices over the past 52 weeks.

title: 7d
description: Stands for data for the past 7 days.

title: 80/20 Rule (Pareto Principle)
description: The 80/20 rule, commonly known as Pareto Principle, states that 20% of your actions account for 80% of results.

title: Accelerator (Startup Accelerator, Seed Accelerators)
description: An accelerator is a time-limited, cohort-based program, supporting startup companies in their business endeavors. The program typically includes mentorship and educational components and is generally concluded by a public pitch event or demo day for potential investors.

title: Accidental Fork
description: An accidental fork is a specific form of a fork which occurs if due to coding errors newer versions of generated blocks are not truly compatible with older ones.

title: Abnormal Return
description: Abnormal return refers to the unusual profits from certain assets or securities over a specific time period.

title: Absolute Advantage
description: Absolute advantage is a situation in which a company can produce the same product as other companies using fewer resources.

title: Absolute Return
description: The term absolute return refers to the return on investment (whether positive or negative) obtained in a specific period of time.

title: Abstract
description: Abstract is something that exists in thought as an idea.

title: Abstraction Scalability
description: Abstraction scalability is the expansion in the overall ability of a system that allows programming components to be used as building blocks in a new development environment.

title: Accepting Risk (Acceptance)
description: Accepting risk, also known as risk acceptance, is a risk management strategy employed by companies to accept risks linked to certain events instead of investing resources to tackle them.

title: Account
description: An account is essentially a record whose purpose is to track the financial activities of a specific asset.

title: Account Abstraction
description: Account abstraction is the process of making it easier for users to interact with blockchain by customizing certain elements of smart contract accounts.

title: Account Balance
description: Account balance refers to the amount in a bank/cryptocurrency account that can be accessed immediately. In accounting, account balance refers to the sum obtained from the difference between all debit and credit transactions posted to a company’s account.

title: Account Number
description: An account number is a string of numbers (and sometimes letters) that is used to identify a specific bank account and the account holder.

title: Accountability
description: Accountability is the requirement or readiness to assume responsibility for one's actions.

title: Accounting Conservatism
description: Accounting conservatism is a principle that necessitates the recognition of future expenses and liabilities immediately in a volatile situation rather than future assets and revenues.

title: Accounting Method
description: An accounting method is a system of rules applied to determine how and when revenue and expenses are recorded in an organization.

title: Accounting Token
description: Accounting tokens are essentially tokenized credit or debit entries (IOU/UOM), just like any spreadsheet-based accounting system.

title: Accredited Investors
description: An accredited investor is a person or organization that is qualified to participate in financial opportunities that are not legally offered to regular investors.

title: Accretion (of a Discount)
description: Accretion of a discount refers to the gain that is generated from the difference between the discounted purchase price and the face value of an asset.

title: Accrual Accounting
description: Accrual accounting is a method in which revenues and expenses are recorded in the year in which they occurred instead of when the payment is actually made.

title: Accrue
description: The accumulated interest, income, or expenses over a period of time is known as an accrual.

title: Accrued Income
description: Accrued income is the type of income that has been earned but is yet to be received, in accordance with the accrual method of accounting.

title: Accrued Interest
description: Accrued interest is the amount of interest owed by or owed to a company on a specified date on a debt or a financial obligation that is yet to be received or paid.

title: Accrued Liabilities
description: Accrued liabilities are financial obligations that have not been credited from the company's bank accounts as invoices for these obligations have not been received.

title: Accrued Revenue
description: Accrued revenue rises when a business records sales without receiving payment for the goods or services sold as they do not invoice the customer at the time of the sale.

title: Accumulation Phase
description: The accumulation phase is a stage in the market cycle right after a downtrend, where institutional investors start buying in tranches, signaling a positive uptrend soon.

title: Accumulation/Distribution Indicator
description: The accumulation/distribution indicator determines the supply and demand level of a stock/asset/cryptocurrency by multiplying the closing price of a specific period with volume.

title: Acid Test Ratio
description: An acid test ratio is a tool that gives an understanding of a company's ability to pay off its current liabilities.

title: Acquisition
description: An acquisition is buying out another company by purchasing a controlling stake.

title: Acquisition Cost
description: Acquisition cost is generally the total cost to be paid for a company’s property, equipment, or other assets after adjusting for incentives, discounts, closing costs, and other necessary expenditures but before the sales taxes.

title: Acquisition Premium
description: Acquisition premium refers to the price difference between the price paid for a company and its assessed market value.

title: Active Management
description: Active management requires a manager, or a team of managers, to actively manage a portfolio.

title: Activist Investor
description: An activist investor is an individual or institution seeking to gain a controlling stake in a company to instigate changes.

title: Address
description: The address is a unique identifier which is created by hashing a public key and describes the digital address where coins or tokens can be transferred to or where coins, tokens, or smart contracts can be stored. A place where cryptocurrency can be sent to and from, in the form of a string of letters and numbers.

title: Adam Back
description: Adam Back is a world-renowned British cryptographer, cypherpunk, and crypto industry figure from the United Kingdom.

title: Adaptive State Sharding
description: Used by Elrond, Adaptive State Sharding is an approach that combines all types of sharding into one to improve communication and performance.

title: Administrative Expenses
description: Administrative expenses are costs incurred by an organization that includes, but are not limited to, benefits and salaries for administrative staff, rent, and managerial compensation.

title: Adoption Curve
description: Adoption curve indicates the pace of adoption of a new technology by people. It may also involve segregation of the target audience to understand the market's willingness.

title: Advance/Decline Line (A/D Line)
description: The A/D line is a technical indicator that plots the difference between the advancing and declining issues in the stock market on a daily basis.

title: Aeternity Blockchain
description: A blockchain network that works on a hybrid consensus approach; both Proof of Work and Proof of Stake.

title: Affiliate
description: An affiliation is a connection between two firms where one company has a small stake in the other company. The concept also applies to the connection between two companies that are owned by the same parent company.

title: Affiliate Marketing
description: Affiliate marketing is a kind of promotion technique where a business pays a person or entity a commission for promoting their goods and services to increase sales.

title: Agency Problem
description: The agency problem, also called the principle-agent problem or the agency dilemma, is the inherent challenge of persuading one party (the agent) to behave in the best interests of another party (the principal) rather than their own benefit.

title: Agency Theory
description: The agency theory discusses how to set up agency relationships in a way that minimizes the likelihood of disputes and other problems arising between agents and principals.

title: Agent
description: An agent is a third party that has been given the legal right to represent a business (the 'principal') and enter into contracts on that business' behalf.

title: Aggregate Demand
description: In an economy, aggregate demand is the total demand for all finished services and goods produced by that economy.

title: Aggressive Investment Strategy
description: An aggressive investment strategy is a high-risk investment strategy that aims to generate the maximum possible returns in financial markets.

title: AI Agents
description: An AI agent is a program or a system that can perceive its environment, make decisions, and take actions independently to achieve a specific goal.

title: AI Coins
description: AI coins are designed to streamline AI-related transactions and interactions, all while upholding transparency and security through blockchain technology.

title: Air Gap
description: If data cannot be accessed, then it cannot be infected or corrupted — this is the concept of an air gap.

title: Airdrop
description: An airdrop is an allocation process for distributing tokens to a group of token holders after an ICO has been completed or a fork has gone live. A marketing campaign that distributes a specific cryptocurrency or token to an audience.

title: Airnode
description: Airnode is an oracle node and API blockchain gateway that is readily deployed by API providers who want to engage in the API3 blockchain protocol and put their data feeds on-chain.

title: Alan Greenspan
description: Alan Greenspan was the former head of the US Federal Reserve and served as the chairman for almost two decades from 1987 to 2006.

title: Algo Trading (Algorithmic Trading)
description: Algo-trading is an automated trading system where buy and sell orders are placed according to the rules of a computer program or algorithm. Algorithmic trading is a trading method which makes use of automated algorithms and pre-programmed trading instructions, such as time, price, and volume to execute tranches of a larger order over time.

title: Algorithm
description: An algorithm is a set of rules or a procedure to be followed for solving a mathematical problem. A process or set of rules to be followed in problem-solving or calculation operations, usually by a computer.

title: Algorithmic Market Operations (AMOs)
description: Algorithmic Market Operations (AMOs) automatically control the supply of algorithmic stablecoins while improving scalability, decentralization, and transparency.

title: Algorithmic Stablecoin
description: An algorithmic stablecoin uses an algorithm underneath, which can issue more coins when its price increases and buy them off the market when the price falls.

title: All Risks Coverage
description: It refers to a type of insurance coverage that automatically covers any risk that the contract does not explicitly omit.

title: All-Time-High (ATH)
description: The highest point (in price, in market capitalization) that a cryptocurrency has been in history. This refers to the highest or lowest the price has ever been for a given asset.

title: All-Time-Low (ATL)
description: An all-time low (ATL) refers to the lowest price a cryptocurrency has hit during its trading history.

title: Allocated Gold
description: Allocated gold refers to a form of gold ownership where the investor physically owns a specific amount of gold stored in a secure vault on their behalf.

title: Allocation
description: Allocation is the allotment of equity or tokens that may be earned, bought, or reserved for a specific team, group, investor, institution, or another similar entity.

title: Allocation Efficiency
description: Allocation efficiency is allocating resources in a manner to optimize the efficiency of the organization.

title: Allotment
description: Allotment refers to the systematic distribution or assignment of resources in a business to various entities over time.

title: Altcoin
description: An Altcoin (umbrella term encompassing 'alt' for 'alternative' and 'coin') is a labeling for a cryptocurrency that is alternative to Bitcoin. As Bitcoin is the first cryptocurrency that captured the world’s imagination, all other coins were subsequently termed 'altcoins,' as in 'alternative coins.' An altcoin refers to a cryptocurrency other than Bitcoin. Each has its own set of rules, properties, and specific use cases. Altcoins could be completely new technologies or forks of existing cryptocurrencies.

title: Altcoin Trader
description: A person who trades cryptocurrencies alternative to Bitcoin.

title: Alternative Investments
description: Alternative investments are assets that have low correlation and can achieve different risk-adjusted returns than traditional equity and fixed income investments.

title: Amalgamation
description: An amalgamation is the merging of two or more organizations that should be recognized as separate legal entities by the jurisdiction’s laws.

title: Amazon S3
description: Amazon Simple Storage Service (S3) is a scalable, high-speed, and inexpensive web-based cloud storage service to store and retrieve data anytime and anywhere.

title: Amended Return
description: An amended return is a revised version of your original tax return.

title: AML (Anti-Money Laundering)
description: AML is a set of measures, laws, and/or regulations designed to prohibit the practice of money laundering. A set of international laws enacted to curtail criminal organizations or individuals laundering money through cryptocurrencies into real-world cash.

title: AMLD5
description: The European Union’s 5th Anti-Money Laundering Directive (AMLD5) is an update to the union’s Anti-Money Laundering (AML) framework.

title: Anarcho-capitalism
description: A political philosophy originally conceived by American economist Murray Rothbard that has now been embraced by many members of the crypto community.

title: Anchoring and Adjustment
description: Anchoring is the psychological phenomenon of having a preconceived idea of something and adjusting your decision-making around that preconceived notion.

title: aNFT (Autonomous NFT)
description: aNFTs (autonomous NFTs) are non-fungible tokens that can be programmed to initiate their own transactions. Every aNFT is a self-contained, self-executing entity that can be designed to do any on-chain action in response to any on and off-chain condition.

title: Angel Investor
description: An angel investor is an affluent or high net worth individual who provides early capital for a business startup, usually in exchange for convertible debt or ownership equity. A person who financially backs a new business venture or startup.

title: Animal Spirits
description: Animal spirits are the driving forces behind the economy that are not purely economic in nature but also include psychological factors, such as confidence and fear.

title: Annual Percentage Rate (APR)
description: The amount of interest a borrower must pay each year is known as the annual percentage rate (APR). The annual percentage rate (APR) is determined by multiplying the periodic interest rate by the number of periods in a year that the periodic rate is used.

title: Annual Percentage Yield (APY)
description: Annual percentage yield (APY) is the rate of return gained over the course of a year on a specific investment. Compounding interest, which is computed on a regular basis and applied to the amount.

title: Annual Report
description: An annual report is an essential document for any company as it provides a detailed understanding of the firm’s financial performance as well as its future prospects.

title: Annualized Rate of Return
description: An annualized rate of return is a way to measure and track the performance of an investment over time.

title: Anonymous
description: Anonymity is when something is not known or named.

title: Anti-dump/Anti-Dumping Policy
description: In the world of blockchain, the anti-dumping policy is a set of rules that protects investors from falling victim to a pump and dump scheme, in which a large number of tokens are purchased by an investor (whale) to boost the token’s value and then dumped at a much higher price, resulting in losses to investors who purchased the asset later.

title: Anti-Fragile
description: A quality attached to an asset that means it performs better when exposed to volatility and shocks.

title: Anti-Malware
description: Anti-Malware is a type of application software that prevents, detects, and removes malware from computers and electronic devices.

title: Anti-Money Laundering (AML)
description: A set of international laws enacted to curtail criminal organizations or individuals laundering money through cryptocurrencies into real-world cash.

title: Antitrust Law
description: Antitrust is a collection of laws that prohibits unfair competition or monopolistic practices by businesses.

title: Antivirus
description: A piece of software designed to protect against malicious software and cyber attacks in general.

title: Antpool
description: Antpool is one of the largest Bitcoin mining pools and aggregates the computing power of many miners to increase the chance of solving a block and receiving the block reward.

title: Apeing
description: Apeing is when a cryptocurrency trader buys a token shortly after the token project launch without conducting thorough research.

title: API (Application Programming Interface)
description: An API is a set of functions and protocols for building application software. It defines methods of communication between various software components and provides access to data of an operating system, application, or other service. API stands for Application Programming Interface. It is a set of routines, protocols, and tools for building software applications. APIs specify how software components should interact, such as what data to use and what actions should be taken.

title: Application Layer
description: The top-most layer of a seven-layered OSI model.

title: AR Token (Arweave)
description: AR is Arweave’s native token.

title: Arbitrage
description: Arbitrage is the practice of quickly buying and selling the same asset in different markets to take advantage of price differences between the markets.

title: Arbitrage Pricing Theory (APT)
description: The arbitrage pricing theory (APT) offers a framework for evaluating market efficiency and identifying arbitrage opportunities in financial markets.

title: Arbitrageur
description: An arbitrageur is a type of investor who exploits pricing inefficiencies between two different markets.

title: Arm Virtual Machine (Qtum)
description: Qtum’s arm virtual machine allows users to execute applications in a decentralized manner.

title: Aroon Indicator
description: Aroon Indicator is used to identify the existence, changes, and corrective retracements and gauge the strength of an ongoing trend in financial markets.

title: Ascending Channel
description: Ascending channels are trend continuation patterns that have an ascending price action as their main characteristic.

title: Ashdraked
description: The complete loss of a trader's total invested capital, specifically as a result of shorting Bitcoin.

title: ASIC (Application-Specific Integrated Circuit)
description: An ASIC is an integrated circuit designed for a specific application, rather than one produced for general purpose. An acronym for application-specific integrated circuit — a device designed for the sole purpose of mining cryptocurrencies.

title: ASIC Miner
description: ASIC mining is the process of mining cryptocurrencies using ASIC equipment allowing for a significantly faster production speed than with an ordinary desktop or laptop as these systems are specifically manufactured for this purpose.

title: ASIC-Resistant
description: This term usually applies to blockchains and mining algorithms, designed to give no benefit for ASICs over consumer-grade hardware.

title: Ask Price
description: The minimum price that a seller is willing to accept for an asset. The ask price is also sometimes referred to as the offer price.

title: Asset
description: Assets are the resources that an organization can use to generate revenue or benefit.

title: Asset Class
description: An asset class is a classification of investments based on common traits, behaviors, and laws.

title: Asset Financing
description: Asset financing emerges as a financial stratagem, allowing enterprises to claim or employ assets by obtaining capital from lenders, such as banks or other financial establishments.

title: Asset Swap
description: An asset swap is a financial transaction where an asset is swapped with another for various purposes.

title: Asset-Backed Tokens
description: Asset-backed tokens are digital claims on a physical asset and are backed by that asset.

title: Asset-Based Approach
description: The asset-based approach takes into account the company's assets for valuation.

title: Asset-Based Lending
description: In asset-based lending, lenders have a vested interest in the value of a company’s assets rather than just its creditworthiness.

title: Assets Under Management (AUM)
description: Assets under management measures the total market values of all the funds controlled by an individual or financial institution on behalf of their clients.

title: Astroturfing
description: The practice of disguising marketing campaigns or otherwise sponsored messaging as the unprompted views of genuine community members.

title: Asynchronous
description: Events that do not occur simultaneously or at the same rate are referred to as asynchronous.

title: Atomic Swap
description: The transfer of cryptocurrency from one party to another, without the use of an exchange or other intermediary.

title: AtomicDEX
description: AtomicDEX offers a cryptocurrency wallet and DEX in one application that is available for multiple platforms.

title: Attestation Ledger
description: An attestation ledger is an account book designed to provide evidence of individual transactions. It is generally used to attest that a financial transaction took place, or to prove the authenticity of transactions or products.

title: Auction
description: An auction is a public sale through a bidding process where an asset is sold to the highest bidder.

title: Audit
description: An audit is a process where developers inspect the underlying code and/or algorithm that compose systems and applications.

title: Auditor
description: An auditor is a trained professional who conducts audits. They are typically employed by accounting firms or work within an organization's internal audit department.

title: Augmented Reality (AR)
description: Augmented Reality (AR) is an immersive experience that improves the value and usage of real-world items using computer-generated intuitive information sent through a variety of sensory modalities such as sound, touch, smell, and sight.

title: Authentication
description: Authentication is a process that confirms a user's identity using passwords, SMS codes, fingerprints, and other forms of ownership proofs before granting access to sensitive and/or personal information.

title: Authority Masternode (VeChain)
description: An authority masternode (AM) is a network-connected server that runs the VeChainThor full node program.

title: Automated Market Maker (AMM)
description: An automated market maker (AMM) is a system that provides liquidity to the exchange it operates in through automated trading.

title: Autonomous Economic Agent (AEA)
description: A solution (software entity) by Fetch.ai and IOTA foundation that can take actions without external input using its own intelligence for the economic benefit of the owner.

title: Average Annual Growth Rate (AAGR)
description: It is the mean return of an individual investment, portfolio, asset, or cash flow on an annual basis.

title: Average Annual Return (AAR)
description: Average Annual Return (AAR) refers to a percentage derived when reporting the historical return.

title: Average Daily Trading Volume (ADTV)
description: The average daily trading volume (ADTV) of stock or crypto is the number of shares/coins traded in one day.

title: Average Directional Index (ADX)
description: The average directional index (ADX) is a technical indicator that measures how strong a market trend is by using price moving averages and is represented by figures ranging between 1 and 100, where a larger value suggests a stronger trend.

title: Average Return
description: The average return is the mean value of a sequence of returns generated over a specified period of time.

title: Average Selling Price (ASP)
description: An average selling price (ASP) refers to the amount at which a specific item is sold.

title: Bag
description: Crypto slang for a large quantity of a specific cryptocurrency. Alternatively (but less frequently) used to refer to the contents of an individual's crypto portfolio.

title: Bagholder
description: An investor who continues to hold large amounts of a specific coin or token, regardless of its performance. A bag holder is a term describing an investor who holds a position in an asset that decreases in value, until virtually worthless with only small chances of regaining value, e.g., holding a cryptocurrency beyond a pump and dump crash.

title: Bail-In
description: Bail-in is a relief or rescue solution offered to a heavily indebted financial institution where the company's depositors, creditors, or bondholders suffer a portion of the company's burden.

title: Bail-Out
description: Bail-out refers to a capital injection or resources given to an entity to prevent a potential downfall, including default and bankruptcy on its financial obligations.

title: Bait and Switch Scam
description: It is a sales strategy in which a customer is attracted to a product or a service at a low rate but is later encouraged to buy one at a higher rate.

title: Bakers
description: Baking is the process that Tezos uses to append new blocks of transactions to its blockchain.

title: Baking
description: Baking is a process that is used by Tezos to append new blocks of transactions onto its blockchain.

title: Balanced Fund
description: Balanced Funds refer to mutual funds containing stock and bond components in a single portfolio.

title: Balanced Investment Strategy
description: A balanced investment strategy attempts to balance the return and risk of a portfolio.

title: Balloon Loan
description: A loan that is not designed to be fully paid back by the end of the term and requires a balloon payment at maturity.

title: Balloon Payment
description: A large amount of money due at the end of the term of a balloon loan is called a balloon payment.

title: Bandwagon Effect
description: The bandwagon effect is the phenomenon in which a person’s decision is influenced by the majority.

title: Bandwidth
description: The quantity of data capacity available for transactional activity on a network is known as bandwidth.

title: Bank for International Settlements (BIS)
description: The BIS is an international financial institution that promotes global monetary stability.

title: Bank Run
description: A bank run occurs when most customers withdraw their cash from the bank out of fear of solvency and bankruptcy.

title: Banking as a Service (BaaS)
description: BaaS platforms provide a higher level of financial transparency options by letting banks open up their APIs for third parties to develop new services.

title: Banking Secrecy Act (BSA)
description: The Bank Secrecy Act (BSA) was implemented in the United States in 1970 to prevent criminals from concealing or laundering their illegal gains.

title: Bankruptcy
description: Bankruptcy is a state where an entity or a person cannot meet its financial obligations, such as debt repayment to creditors upon legal declaration.

title: Bar Chart
description: A bar chart is a graph used for data visualization and technical analysis in finance. It consists of vertical and horizontal lines arranged on a graph to give meaningful information.

title: Bear
description: Someone who believes that prices in a given market will decline over an extended period. Such a person might be referred to as 'bearish.' A bear market describes the phenomenon where market prices are generally trending downward over a given period and public perception is negative.

title: Bear Call Spread
description: A vertical spread with two calls of different rates but at the same expiration date is known as a bear call spread.

title: Bear Hug
description: A hostile takeover strategy, where a company is offered to be bought at a higher than target rate.

title: Bear Market
description: When prices of assets in a market fall by 20% or more from recent highs, it is called a bear market. As a result, investor confidence is low, and the economy and market turn pessimistic. A bear market is the reverse of a bull market, where outlooks are negative as market prices seem to be on a downward trend.

title: Bear Trap
description: The attempted manipulation of a specific cryptocurrency’s price, based on the coordinated activity of a group of traders.

title: Bearwhale
description: A bearwhale is a person who has a high number of cryptocurrencies and uses their massive account to drive the price down and profit out of it.

title: Behavioral Finance
description: Behavioral finance is a branch of finance that combines psychology and economics to explain how human emotions and cognitive biases influence financial decisions.

title: Benchmark
description: Benchmarking is a method of comparing the performance of your asset or investment portfolio to that of similar assets to see whether there is a gap that can be bridged by increasing performance indicators.

title: Benchmark Index
description: A benchmark index is a popular index security that is used as a gauge or benchmark, against which the progress of the broader market may be tracked.

title: Benefit-Cost Ratio
description: The benefit-cost ratio (BCR) is a profitability indicator used in cost-benefit analysis to determine the viability of cash flows generated from an asset or project.

title: BEP-2 (Binance Chain Tokenization Standard)
description: A technical standard for tokens on Binance Chain.

title: BEP-20
description: BEP-20 is a BNB Chain token standard created with the intention of extending ERC-20.

title: BEP-721
description: BEP-721 is a Binance Smart Chain (BSC) token standard that enables the generation of non-fungible tokens (NFTs). It is considered to be an extension of ERC-721 that is one of the most popular NFT standards.

title: BEP-95 (Bruno Hard Fork Upgrade)
description: Binance Evolution Protocol (BEP-95) is a Bruno hard fork upgrade that aims to speed up the BNB token burning process.

title: Beta (Release)
description: A software pre-release stage where its access is offered to a set number of users and third-party software testers for testing it under real-world settings.

title: Bid-Ask Spread
description: Bid-ask spread is the difference between the highest price which a buyer is willing to pay for an asset as well as the lowest price that a seller is willing to accept. See spread.

title: Bid Price
description: The cost that someone is willing to pay for a security, asset, commodity, service, or contract is referred to as a bid price.

title: Big Tech
description: The four or five biggest technological corporations, particularly Facebook, Apple, Google, and Amazon, are referred to as 'Big Tech' as they enjoy the biggest shares in their respective industries.

title: Binance Chain Explorer
description: Binance Chain Explorer is a web-based platform that provides access to information and data related to the BNB Chain.

title: Binance Labs
description: Binance Labs is a project to nurture, invest in, and develop blockchain and cryptocurrency businesses, initiatives, and communities, as well as a social impact fund.

title: Binance Launchpad
description: Binance Launchpad offers crypto-startups a platform to raise capital and market their projects to millions of crypto investors in the Binance ecosystem.

title: Binary Code
description: Binary code is a two-symbol system that is based on numbers, '0' and '1,' to represent text, computer processor commands, or any other type of data.

title: Bit
description: A bit is a basic unit of information in computing.

title: Bitcoin
description: Bitcoin is a blockchain-based cryptocurrency and a digital payment system invented by an unknown individual or group with the alias Satoshi Nakamoto in 2008.

title: Bitcoin 3.0
description: Bitcoin 3.0 represents the next evolutionary phase of Bitcoin mining, characterized by its potential to become a responsible partner in both the energy and financial ecosystems.

title: Bitcoin ATM (BTM)
description: An automated teller machine (ATM or cashpoint) that allows the user to buy and sell Bitcoin.

title: Bitcoin Cash (BCH)
description: Bitcoin Cash is a cryptocurrency resulting from a hard fork of Bitcoin which took effect on 1 August 2017 in order to increase Bitcoin's block sizes.

title: Bitcoin Compounding
description: Bitcoin compounding is the process of growing Bitcoin holdings by converting any rewards or yield earned across both centralized and decentralized networks back into BTC and reinvesting them.

title: Bitcoin DApps
description: Decentralized applications (DApps) running on Bitcoin-powered blockchains while benefiting from the core features of the Bitcoin network are known as Bitcoin DApps.

title: Bitcoin Dominance (BTCD)
description: Bitcoin Dominance is a metric that determines how much share of the overall crypto market share is owned by Bitcoin.

title: Bitcoin ETF
description: A Bitcoin ETF, or exchange-traded fund, is a type of investment fund that tracks the price of Bitcoin and allows investors to buy and sell shares of the fund on an exchange.

title: Bitcoin Halving
description: Bitcoin halving is an event in which the total rewards per confirmed block halves. The reduction of the block reward for Bitcoin mining by 50%. A halving occurs after 210,000 blocks have been mined since the last, which corresponds to roughly every 4 years. The Bitcoin network started with a 50 BTC block reward and currently, after 3 halvings, it is at a level of 6.25 BTC per block.

title: Bitcoin Improvement Proposal (BIP)
description: The standard format for documents proposing changes to Bitcoin.

title: Bitcoin Misery Index (BMI)
description: The Bitcoin Misery Index (BMI) is used by investors as an investment tool that ranges from 0 to 100.

title: Bitcoin NFTs
description: Non-fungible tokens (NFTs) minted on Bitcoin-powered blockchains and secured by the Bitcoin network are known as Bitcoin NFTs.

title: Bitcoin Pizza
description: Bitcoin Pizza refers to the infamous transaction where a guy, named Laszlo Hanyecz, paid 10,000 Bitcoins for two pizzas making it the first business transaction of Bitcoin in the real world.

title: Bitcoin Virtual Machine (BitVM)
description: BitVM, or Bitcoin Virtual Machine, is a proposed system described in a whitepaper by Robin Linus that allows complex computations and smart contracts to be executed on the Bitcoin network.

title: Bitcoiner
description: A person who is bullish on Bitcoin.

title: Bitcointalk
description: Bitcointalk is the most popular online forum dedicated to Bitcoin, cryptocurrency, and blockchain technology.

title: BitLicense
description: A business license permitting regulated virtual currency activities, issued by the New York State Department of Financial Services.

title: BitPay
description: BitPay is a Bitcoin payment service provider.

title: Bits
description: A commonly used unit, or subdivision, of a single Bitcoin.

title: Bitstream
description: Configuration data loaded onto an FPGA is called Bitstream.

title: Black Hat Hacker
description: Black hat hackers usually use malware to penetrate into computerized networks and systems to steal data.

title: Black Swan Event
description: A black swan event, also known as black swan occurrences, is a metaphor for an unexpected event that has a significant impact.

title: Black-Scholes Model
description: In simple terms, it is a mathematical formula that gives the fair price of stock options, allowing investors to calculate whether they are overvalued or undervalued.

title: Blake-256
description: Used in Decred, Blake-256 is a hash algorithm designed by Jean-Philippe Aumasson, Luca Henzen, Willi Meier, and Raphael C.-W. Phan.

title: Block
description: A block is an unalterable set of data making up a pivotal component of the blockchain by recording the most recent transactions that have not yet been recorded by any previous block. A file containing information on transactions completed during a given time period. Blocks are the constituent parts of a blockchain. Blockchains are built up of a growing list of transactions, which are formatted into blocks. Each block contains a cryptographic reference to the last, making it impossible to change the history of the blockchain.

title: Block Explorer
description: An application enabling a user to view details of blocks on a given blockchain. Also known as a blockchain browser.

title: Block Header
description: A block header is a unique identifier for a block on a blockchain that is hashed on a continuous basis to supply proof-of-work for mining incentives.

title: Block Height
description: The block height is a metric used to describe the number of sequentially added blocks in the blockchain between any given block and the genesis block. A value describing the number of blocks preceding a given block in the blockchain.

title: Block Lattice (Nano)
description: The block lattice is a data structure that replaces the traditional blockchain used by most cryptocurrencies with a network of individual blockchains, one for each user.

title: Block Producer
description: A block producer (BP) is a person or group whose hardware is chosen to verify a block's transactions and begin the next block on most Proof-of-Stake (PoS) blockchains.

title: Block Reward
description: The coins awarded to a miner or group of miners for solving the cryptographic problem required to create a new block on a given blockchain. When blocks are created by miners or validators, a block reward is issued. This takes the form of newly minted cryptocurrency, which is used as an incentive for participants to help keep the blockchain running.

title: Block Size
description: In blockchain technology, block size refers to the amount of data about transactions a single block in the chain can carry.

title: Block Time
description: Block time refers to the approximate time it takes for a blockchain-based system to produce a new block.

title: Block Trade
description: A block trade is a large-scale purchase or sale of securities that occur outside of an open market. It uses blockhouse as a financial intermediary to aid investors with risk management.

title: Blockchain
description: A distributed ledger system. A sequence of blocks, or units of digital information, stored consecutively in a public database. The basis for cryptocurrencies. The blockchain is a publicly accessible distributed ledger that was initially designed and implemented to enable Bitcoin transactions. It is a piece of IT infrastructure that serves as a database which is used to keep a continuously growing list of records, so-called blocks. Blockchains are a method of storing data in a decentralized way. The data can be duplicated and distributed across the network of computer systems on that blockchain. Usually operated by a community of miners or validators, blockchains don’t require a centralized intermediary to operate.

title: Blockchain 1.0
description: Blockchain 1.0 is the first generation of blockchain technology, which focuses on cryptocurrency and decentralization.

title: Blockchain 2.0
description: Blockchain 2.0 is an extension to blockchain 1.0 as it introduced the concept of decentralization of business and markets through smart contracts and improved security and transparency.

title: Blockchain 3.0
description: Blockchain 3.0 is the final developmental stage of blockchain technology, which predicts global, institutional, and enterprise adoption.

title: Blockchain Explorer
description: A blockchain explorer is simply a search engine allowing users to browse through blockchain records.

title: Blockchain Mutual Credit
description: Blockchain mutual credit is a framework within which stable cryptocurrencies can be derived from multilateral exchange networks.

title: Blockchain Transmission Protocol (BTP)
description: Blockchain Transmission Protocol (BTP) enables isolated blockchains to operate as a fully decentralized settlement layer by securely anchoring transactions using a protocol that is universal.

title: Blockchain Tribalism
description: Blockchain tribalism refers to people in the blockchain or crypto community becoming ideologically aligned with a specific blockchain or crypto.

title: Blockchain Trilemma
description: The blockchain trilemma is the set of three issues that plague blockchains: decentralization, security, and scalability.

title: Blockchain-As-a-Service (BaaS)
description: BaaS offers the capabilities of blockchain technology to businesses but without the necessity of establishing and maintaining a dedicated blockchain framework.

title: Blockchain-Enabled Smart Locks
description: Blockchain-enabled smart locks solve many security issues and can be locked or unlocked based on the state of a variable that is embedded in a smart contract.

title: Blockweave
description: Blockweave is a data storage protocol that builds upon blockchain architecture. It utilizes a unique interconnected structure linking each block to the previous block and a random older block.

title: Bluesky Crypto Protocol
description: The Bluesky crypto protocol is a decentralized social network protocol, organized by Twitter, that allows several social networks to interact with other social networks, thanks to an open standard.

title: Bollinger Band
description: A tool developed by Bollinger to help in the recognition of systemic pattern recognition in prices; it is a band that is plotted two standard deviations away from the simple moving average, or exponential moving average in some cases.

title: Bonding Curve
description: A bonding curve is a mathematical curve that defines the relationship between the price and the supply of a given asset.

title: Bots
description: Automated software that can carry out tasks such as cryptocurrency trades.

title: Bottleneck
description: A bottleneck refers to a point where capacity becomes restricted, creating congestion and slowing the overall performance.

title: Bounty
description: A cryptocurrency bounty is a reward users receive for performing tasks assigned by a given blockchain or project.

title: Brave Browser
description: Brave Browser is a privacy-focused web browser that blocks ads and trackers by default.

title: BRC-20
description: Taking its cue from Ethereum's ERC-20, the Bitcoin blockchain's BRC-20 is an experimental token standard. Ordinals protocol supports the generation and transfer of fungible tokens.

title: Breaking
description: In the world of cryptocurrencies, breaking the forward compatibility of cryptoassets is seen in hard forks of a cryptocurrency.

title: Brian Armstrong
description: Brian Armstrong is the founder of Coinbase, one of the largest cryptocurrency exchanges in the United States.

title: Bridges
description: A blockchain bridge allows the seamless transfer of data or tokens between two different blockchain projects.

title: Browser Extension
description: A browser extension is a plugin for an internet browser that adds additional features.

title: Brute Force Attack (BFA)
description: An attempt to crack a password or key through automated trial and error.

title: Bubble
description: When an asset is traded at a price exceeding that asset's intrinsic value.

title: Bull
description: A person that is optimistic and confident that market prices will increase, this person is also known to be 'bullish' about the market or price. A bull market describes the phenomenon where market prices are generally trending upward over a given period and public perception is positive.

title: Bull Market
description: A bull market in crypto and stock markets refers to a time during which the prices of assets grow dramatically. These markets act as a source of motivation for both investors and purchasers. This is not a permanent state, although it can linger for months or even years.

title: Bull Run
description: A bull run (also known as a bull trend) is a period of time in the financial market during which the values of certain assets are constantly rising.

title: Bull Trap
description: A bull trap occurs when a steadily declining asset appears to reverse and go upward, but soon resumes its downward trend.

title: Burn/Burned
description: Cryptocurrency tokens or coins are considered 'burned' when they have been purposely and permanently removed from circulation. See coin burn.

title: Burn Reserve
description: A burn reserve describes the lot of tokens that are not distributed during ICO and which will be burned in order to avoid dilution.

title: Buy The (F*******) Dip (BTD/BTFD)
description: An enthusiastic exclamation by supporters of a cryptocurrency to buy while prices are at a low point.

title: Buy Wall
description: A buy wall is a disproportionately large buy limit order placed on a cryptocurrency exchange.

title: Byron Phase
description: Byron Phase is the first phase of Cardano that was released in September 2017.

title: Byzantine Fault (Error Avalanche)
description: The Byzantine fault is a condition of a distributed computing system where components may fail, yet imperfect information exists on whether a component has failed and if so which one(s).

title: Byzantine Fault Tolerance
description: Byzantine fault tolerance is the ability of a computing system to cope with the questionable reliability of data caused by the Byzantine fault.

title: Byzantine Generals’ Problem
description: A situation where communication that requires consensus on a single strategy from all members within a group or party cannot be trusted or verified.

title: Byzantium Fork
description: The Byzantium hard fork was geared towards making Ethereum's smart contracts suitable for usage in the commercial space and to increase the speed of the transactions with an enhancement in the security on its blockchain.

title: C++
description: C++ is an extension of the C programming language that allows cross-platform developments and capabilities.

title: Call Options
description: Call options are financial contracts that give an option buyer the right, but not the obligation, to purchase a stock, bond, commodity, or other asset at a specific price.

title: Candlesticks
description: A candlestick chart is a graphing technique used to show changes in price over time. Each candle provides 4 points of information: opening price, closing price, high, and low. Also known as 'candles' for short.

title: Capital
description: Capital is most commonly defined as the large sum of money you would use to invest.

title: Capital Efficiencies
description: Capital efficiency is the ratio that compares the spending of a company on their growing revenue and how much they are receiving in return in the way of profits.

title: Capital Funds
description: Capital funding is the money provided in the form of debt or equity to operate a company.

title: Capitulation
description: Capitulation is the process of selling assets or cryptocurrencies at a significant loss because you have lost hope or belief that it will ever increase in price.

title: Casascius Coin
description: A physical unit of Bitcoin that comes in the form of brass, silver, or gold-plated coins.

title: Cascading Liquidations
description: Cascading liquidation refers to an event where liquidations pile on top of each other, resulting in a sudden price change.

title: Cash
description: Cash is the most liquid form of money: physical coins and banknotes in the most narrow sense of the term.

title: CashToken
description: CashTokens are a new set of opcodes that extend the capabilities of Bitcoin Cash to support new financial primitives in the form of fungible and non-fungible tokens.

title: Casper (Ethereum)
description: Casper is a project designed to implement PoS into the Ethereum network.

title: Cathie Wood
description: Cathie Wood is a top stock investor and the founder of ARK Invest, a $60 billion (assets) firm that invests in cutting-edge technologies, including self-driving vehicles and genomics.

title: CeDeFi
description: CeDeFi, or centralized decentralized finance, combines traditional centralized financial services with decentralized applications, merging conventional regulatory policies with modern financial products and infrastructure.

title: Censorship
description: Censorship is the act of altering, suppressing, or prohibiting speech or writing that is considered detrimental to the general public.

title: Censorship Resistance
description: Censorship resistance refers to the idea that no party can prevent anyone from participating in a given platform or network.

title: Central Bank
description: In contemporary economies, the central bank is responsible for the formulation and transmission of monetary policy, as well as for the regulation of member banks.

title: Central Bank Digital Currency (CBDC)
description: CBDCs are digital currencies issued by a central bank whose status as legal tender depends on government regulation or law.

title: Central Ledger (Centralized Ledger, General Ledger)
description: A central ledger is a single principal repository that keeps records of transactions of an entity and is typically controlled by a single person or a narrowly defined group of individuals. A central ledger is a physical book or a computer file used to record transactions in a centralized manner.

title: Central Limit Order Books (CLOBs)
description: Central Limit Order Books (CLOBs) are trading systems, widely used in traditional finance (TradFi), that match buy and sell orders based on price and time priority.

title: Central Processing Unit (CPU)
description: A central processing unit (CPU) is the part of a computer that is in charge of interpreting and executing programs and coordinating the work of all other components.

title: Centralized
description: A centralized organizational structure is one in which a single node or a small number of them are in control of an entire network.

title: Centralized Exchange (CEX)
description: Centralized exchanges (CEXs) are a type of cryptocurrency exchange that is operated by a company that owns it in a centralized manner.

title: Centre (Consortium)
description: A member-based consortium by Coinbase and Circle to manage USD Coin (USDC).

title: Certificate of Deposit (CD)
description: A certificate of deposit (CD) is a financial product allowing customers to earn an interest rate premium after making a deposit.

title: Chain Reorganization
description: Chain reorganization is a process in blockchain technology that allows node operators to replace blocks and adopt new ones, in order to create new, longer chains of data.

title: Chain Split
description: Chain splits are another term used to describe cryptocurrency forks — the separation of a single original coin into several independently managed projects.

title: Change
description: Change — a concept relevant to cryptocurrencies that use the UTXO model — is the number of coins sent back to a user after they use their unspent outputs to initiate a transaction.

title: Change Address
description: In cryptocurrencies, a change address is where the change from a transaction is temporarily stored before it is returned to the sender wallet.

title: Changpeng Zhao (CZ)
description: Changpeng Zhao (CZ) is the founder of crypto exchange Binance.

title: Chargeback
description: A chargeback is the return of money to the payer of a certain transaction, most commonly one that was made with a credit or debit card.

title: Chicago Mercantile Exchange (CME)
description: The Chicago Mercantile Exchange (CME) is one of the largest exchanges dealing in the trading of futures and options in the United States.

title: Chunk (NEAR)
description: Chunk is a fraction of each block produced as a result of sharding in the NEAR protocol.

title: Cipher
description: A cipher is any algorithm that can be used to encrypt and decrypt information.

title: Ciphertext
description: Ciphertext is a result of encryption that has been performed on plaintext through the usage of an algorithm.

title: Circle
description: Circle is the Fintech firm behind USDC.

title: Circulating Supply
description: The best approximation of the number of coins that are circulating in the market and in the general public’s hands. The circulating supply is the amount of coins that are circulating in the market, i.e., which are in the general public's hand; if tokens are pre-mined the circulating supply will equal the hard cap after ICO.

title: Client
description: A client is software that can access and process blockchain transactions on a local computer. A common application of this is a cryptocurrency software wallet.

title: Close
description: Refers to the closing price; similar to the same term used in stock trading.

title: Cloud
description: Cloud servers are typically located throughout different data centers all over the world.

title: Cloud Mining
description: Cryptocurrency mining with remote processing power rented from companies.

title: Co-Signer
description: A person or entity that has partial control and access over a cryptocurrency wallet.

title: Code
description: The action of coding is to write programming statements for a program.

title: Code Repository
description: A code repository is a digital library where developers store and collaborate on their codes with ease, like a code-specific Google Drive or Dropbox.

title: Coin
description: Coin is a commonly used alternative expression for cryptocurrencies. A coin can refer to a cryptocurrency that can operate independently or to a single unit of such cryptocurrency.

title: Coin Mixer
description: Coin mixers allow users to mix up transactions between different cryptocurrency addresses, so they become untraceable and cannot be followed back to the initial sender or receiver of the assets.

title: Coin-Margined Trading
description: Coin-margined trading is a form of trading where cryptocurrencies or any other form of digital asset serves as the base for all transaction and settlement purposes.

title: Coinbase
description: In mineable cryptocurrencies, a coinbase is the number of coins that are generated from scratch and awarded to miners for mining every new block.

title: Coinbase Transaction
description: The first transaction in a new block is a coinbase transaction in which the miner receives Bitcoins and mining fees.

title: Cold Storage
description: Offline storage of cryptocurrencies, typically involving hardware non-custodial wallets, USBs, offline computers, or paper wallets.

title: Cold Wallet
description: A cryptocurrency wallet that is in cold storage, i.e., not connected to the internet. See cold storage.

title: Collaborative Venture Building (CVB)
description: Collaborative venture building (CVB) is a process where multiple individuals or organizations come together to create a new company or collaborate on a product.

title: Collateral
description: Collateral is any asset that a lender accepts as a form of security to ensure that the borrower repays a loan.

title: Collateral Cap
description: Collateral cap is a security feature designed to diversify protocol-wide lending risk away from any one asset.

title: Collateral Factor
description: Collateral Factor is the maximum amount a user can borrow, represented in percentages, based on the total amount of assets supplied.

title: Collateral Margin
description: The collateral margin is the percentage of the total value of the investment that the investor must contribute in their own funds, while the remaining portion is financed by the broker.

title: Collateral Tokens
description: In cryptocurrency, collateral tokens are used as a risk mitigation asset when borrowing other types of crypto tokens.

title: Collateralization
description: Collateralization is the process of using one asset as insurance for securing a loan in a different asset.

title: Collateralized Debt Obligation
description: A collateralized debt obligation (CDO) represents a mixture of loans and assets that are offered to big investment firms with a lot of capital.

title: Collateralized Debt Position (CDP)
description: A collateralized debt position is held by locking collateral in smart contracts to generate stablecoins.

title: Collateralized Mortgage Obligation (CMO)
description: A collateralized mortgage obligation (CMO) is essentially a bundle of numerous mortgages combined in a package and sold to investors.

title: Collateralized Stablecoin
description: A 'collateralized stablecoin' is a stablecoin that is entirely or almost entirely backed by collateral held in a reserve.

title: Commingling
description: Commingling of funds is a method of combining all funds from different investors into a single investment in order to maximize the benefits.

title: Commodity Futures Trading Commission (CFTC)
description: The Commodity Futures Trading Commission (CFTC) is an independent federal regulatory agency responsible for regulating the U.S. derivatives market.

title: Community Takeover (CTO)
description: Community Takeover (CTO) in crypto refers to a situation where the original creators or developers of a crypto project abandon or exit the project, but the community of users and token holders steps in to take control of its development and future direction.

title: COMP Token
description: The native asset of the Compound protocol.

title: Composability (DeFi)
description: In DeFi, composability allows developers to combine different components of blockchain to create new applications and services.

title: Composable DeFi
description: Composable DeFi refers to the interoperability between different DeFi protocols. It enables a multitude of DeFi applications to work along and create a wide range of new use cases and financial products.

title: Composable Token
description: A composable token is an ERC-998 token, a standard extension to any non-fungible token, adding the ability for non-fungible tokens to own other non-fungible (ERC-721) and fungible (ERC-20) tokens.

title: Concentrated Liquidity
description: Concentrated liquidity greatly improves the capital efficiency for LPs and opens up the door to a whole number of liquidity provision strategies in the process.

title: Confidential Computing
description: At its core, it's a technology that enables data to remain encrypted and protected even while being actively used for calculations and analysis.

title: Confirmation
description: In cryptocurrency, a confirmation is a measure of how many blocks have actually passed since a transaction was added to a blockchain.

title: Confirmations
description: A cryptocurrency transaction is considered confirmed when it is included in a block on the blockchain. Each new block after the first one is an additional confirmation for that transaction.

title: Consensus
description: Consensus is achieved when all participants of the network agree on the order and content of the blocks in the blockchain. Consensus, in the context of cryptocurrencies, refers to the method by which blockchain participants agree on what should be included into the blockchain. The two most prominent consensus mechanisms currently used by crypto networks today are proof-of-work and proof-of-stake.

title: Consensus Layer
description: The consensus layer is the backbone of any blockchain network, performing the vital role of facilitating agreement among nodes on the true state of the blockchain.

title: Consensus Mechanism
description: A consensus mechanism is an underlying technology behind the main functionalities of all blockchain technology, which makes them an essential operating feature of all cryptocurrencies. A consensus mechanism describes the actions necessary to achieve agreement on data in distributed systems.

title: ConsenSys
description: ConsenSys is a blockchain technology company that offers developer tools alongside enterprise solutions.

title: Consolidation
description: Consolidation in trading is when a crypto asset trades between two levels, and the market shows indecisiveness about the next move.

title: Consortium Blockchain
description: A privately owned and operated blockchain where a consortium shares information not readily available to the public, while relying on the immutable and transparent properties of the blockchain.

title: Consumer Crypto
description: Consumer Crypto refers to blockchain-based applications and services that are easy to use and seamlessly integrate into everyday life, regardless of technical expertise.

title: Consumer Price Index (CPI)
description: A Consumer Price Index (or CPI) is a type of index where the prices of a basket of goods and services are tracked to gain insights into market segments.

title: Contract
description: In traditional finance, a contract is a binding agreement between two parties. In cryptocurrencies, smart contracts execute functions on the blockchain.

title: Contract Account
description: A contract account is an account that has a crypto balance and associated code.

title: Contract for Difference (CFD)
description: A contract for difference (CFD) outlines a buyer’s obligation to pay any price difference that might occur due to the shifting valuation of an asset.

title: Coordinator
description: In blockchain technology, a coordinator is a specialized client that allows nodes to verify the validity of their copy of the ledger against specific transactions.

title: Core Wallet
description: A core crypto wallet is able to contain the entire blockchain, rather than just a piece of a blockchain.

title: Corporate Treasury
description: A corporate treasury is formed to manage and control the liquidity, risk, funds, capital reserves, and other resources of a company to align with its short and long-term strategies.

title: Correction
description: A correction is a pullback of an asset’s price of at least 10% to adjust for over-valuation.

title: Counter-Terrorism Financing
description: Counter-terrorism financing refers to efforts to disrupt and cut off the money supply used to fund terrorist organizations and activities.

title: CPU Miner
description: Since mining requires computing power, the process of generating or mining cryptocurrency using a central processing unit (CPU) is called CPU mining (or central processing unit mining).

title: Craig Wright
description: Craig Wright is an Australian computer scientist associated with Bitcoin SV.

title: Credit Rating
description: Credit rating is a measure that allows banks and lending institutions to predict how capable you are of repaying your debt.

title: Credit Risk
description: Credit risk is the number representing the possibility that a bank or lending institution will lose money because a borrower cannot repay their loan.

title: Cross Margin
description: Cross Margin, also known as 'Spread Margin,' is a margin method that utilizes the full amount of funds in the available balance to avoid liquidations. Any realized profit & loss statement (P&L) from other positions can aid in adding margin on a losing position.

title: Cross-Border Trading
description: Cross-border trading in financial markets and trade finance represents the opportunity to trade globally using a local currency.

title: Cross-Chain
description: Cross-chain is a technology that enhances the interconnection between blockchain networks by allowing the exchange of information and value.

title: Cross-Chain Communication
description: Cross-chain communication between blockchains allows different protocols to verify data and transactions without the intervention of a centralized third-party service.

title: Cross-Chain Contract Calls
description: Cross-chain contract calls allow information, cryptocurrencies, or NFTs, which would otherwise be constrained to their own network, to move freely between blockchains via smart contracts.

title: Crowdfunding
description: Crowdfunding enables fundraisers to collect money from a large number of people through a variety of different platforms. Crowdfunding is one specific form of crowdsourcing where funding for a project or business is raised from a number of people, oftentimes via campaigns launched via the Internet.

title: Crowdloan
description: The practice of new projects to raise funds through DOT or KSM tokens for slots on Kusama or Polkadot network.

title: Crypto Debit Card
description: A crypto debit card is a type of debit card that allows its holder to pay for goods and services using cryptocurrencies.

title: Crypto Invoicing
description: Crypto invoicing is the process of creating invoices for goods and services that need to be paid in cryptocurrencies.

title: Crypto Loan
description: A crypto loan is a type of secured loan, similar to an auto or student loan, in which you commit to an asset as collateral in order to secure financing.

title: Crypto Winter
description: Crypto winter is a period in the crypto market when prices of major coins fall dramatically from all-time highs.

title: Cryptoasset
description: A cryptoasset is any digital asset that uses cryptographic technologies to maintain its operation as a currency or decentralized application.

title: Cryptocurrency (Math-Based Currency)
description: Cryptocurrencies are digital currencies that use cryptographic technologies to secure their operation. A cryptocurrency is a digital currency in which encryption techniques are used to control the generation of units of currency and verify the transfer of funds, operating independently of one single central unit. Cryptocurrency businesses oftentimes raise money through ICOs.

title: Cryptocurrency Exchange (Crypto Exchange, Digital Currency Exchange)
description: A cryptocurrency exchange is a platform that provides users with the possibility to trade cryptocurrencies for other cryptocurrencies and/or fiat money. Businesses that allow customers to trade cryptocurrencies for fiat money or other cryptocurrencies.

title: Cryptocurrency Money Laundering
description: Cryptocurrency money laundering is a method criminals use to legitimize and enshroud funds by changing fiat to digital currency and then routing it through many pathways. It is an attempt to lose any authorities who may be tracing the money.

title: Cryptocurrency Pairs
description: Exchanges utilize cryptocurrency pairs in order to facilitate the trade between different tokens.

title: Cryptography
description: A field of study and practice to secure information, preventing third parties from reading information to which they are not privy. Cryptography refers to the science of keeping information secure and safe, and is used in many areas in computing today. Cryptography helps secure blockchains and cryptocurrencies through the SHA-256 one-way hashing algorithm to enable Proof-of-Work, and through private-key/public-key cryptography for authenticating and validating cryptocurrency transfers.

title: Cryptojacking
description: The use of another party’s computer to mine cryptocurrency without their consent.

title: Cryptology
description: Cryptology is the scientific study of cryptography as well as cryptanalysis.

title: CryptoPunks
description: A collection of Ethereum-based non-fungible tokens.

title: Currency
description: Currency is a medium of exchange that defines value.

title: Currency Crisis
description: A currency crisis is termed as a financial emergency in which a country's fiat currency loses value, and investors become cautious of retaining/investing in that country's assets.

title: Curve AMO
description: Curve is a software that uses multiple cryptocurrencies to operate an automated market maker (AMM) service focused on stablecoins (cryptocurrencies programmed to mimic other assets).

title: Custodial
description: Custodial cryptocurrency businesses are the ones that are in possession of their customers’ funds for the duration of the use of their services.

title: Custodian
description: A custodian is a person or entity who has responsibility for taking care of assets. A custodian is responsible for safely holding assets for an institution or individual for a variety of purposes.

title: Custody
description: Custody is a financial institution's legal capacity to keep and preserve financial assets for its clients to avoid asset theft or loss.

title: Cypherpunk
description: The cypherpunk movement promotes the use of cryptography and other privacy-focused technologies to advance social and political progress. A cypherpunk is a person that actively advocates the widespread usage of cryptography and privacy-enhancing technologies as a means to social and political change.

title: Daedalus Wallet
description: Daedalus Wallet is a multi-platform, open-source, hierarchical-deterministic wallet that lets you generate an endless number of keys from a single seed.

title: DAO (Decentralized Autonomous Organization)
description: A DAO, or decentralized autonomous organization, refers to a business entity that is represented as transparent rules in a number of smart contracts. They aim to reduce centralization as much as possible, typically giving the community the ability to govern the future of the DAO and its products by voting for or against any proposed changes or updates. The DAO (Decentralized Autonomous Organization) was an organization set up to pool funds to develop technologies supporting new decentralized business models; after being hacked in June 2016 it ceased to exist in late 2016.

title: DAO Summoning
description: DAO summoning is the act of creating or forming a DAO. The term is typically used in the context of forming a new Moloch DAO, though it can also refer to the formation of any new DAO.

title: Dark Web
description: A portion of the internet existing on darknets not indexed by search engines, that can only be accessed with specific software, configurations, or authorizations.

title: Darknodes
description: RenVM is driven by Darknodes, a decentralized network of computers. In exchange for compensation, they offer their computing power and storage space to everyone with certain conditions.

title: Data Availability Sampling (DAS)
description: Data Availability Sampling (DAS) is a method that enables decentralized applications to verify the availability of block data without requiring the entire dataset to be downloaded by each participant.

title: Data Privacy
description: Data privacy refers to the area of data protection and security that is responsible for the handling of sensitive data.

title: Data Scraping
description: Data scraping or web scraping is the process of extracting information from a website into a spreadsheet or a local file on your computer or database.

title: Data Validation
description: Data validation is the process of clarifying the accuracy, integrity, and quality of a set of data before it is used.

title: Date of Launch
description: A term used for when ICOs will put up their tokens for sale.

title: Day Trading
description: Day trading is the practice of frequently buying and selling assets in order to make a profit on intraday changes in their price.

title: Dead Cat Bounce
description: A temporary recovery in prices after a prolonged decrease.

title: Dead Coin
description: A cryptocurrency that is no longer in existence.

title: Death Cross
description: A death cross is a bearish technical trading indicator that occurs when the 50-day moving average falls below the 200-day moving average, indicating a big sell-off.

title: Decentralization Maximalism
description: Decentralization maximalism refers to the belief that decentralization is the best approach and lifestyle to such a degree that any form of regulation does not need to exist.

title: Decentralization Ratio
description: The Decentralization Ratio (DR) is the ratio of collateral value that is decentralized over the total stablecoin supply backed for those assets.

title: Decentralized
description: Decentralization refers to the property of a system in which nodes or actors work in concert in a distributed fashion to achieve a common goal. Decentralized is a word that is used to describe technologies that make use of distributed systems with the aim to provide increased security and redundancy, and to lessen a reliance on governing bodies and centralized intermediaries.

title: Decentralized API (dAPI)
description: API services that are intrinsically interoperable with blockchain technology are known as decentralized application programming interfaces (dAPIs). This is an invention of the API3 protocol.

title: Decentralized Applications (DApps)
description: A type of application that runs on a decentralized network, avoiding a single point of failure. dApps, or decentralized applications, are programs that run on top of blockchain networks and use smart contracts to provide trustless tools and services for end users. The most widely adopted smart contract platform today is the Ethereum protocol, where hundreds of dApps exist today.

title: Decentralized Autonomous Initial Coin Offerings (DAICO)
description: A method for decentralized funding of projects that introduces a form of governance in the ICO process, allowing backers to vote for the return of their funds if certain conditions are met.

title: Decentralized Autonomous Organizations (DAO)
description: A decentralized autonomous organization (DAO) is founded upon and governed by a set of computer-defined rules and blockchain-based smart contracts.

title: Decentralized Currency
description: Decentralized currency refers to bank-free methods of transferring wealth or ownership of any other commodity without needing a third party.

title: Decentralized Database
description: A decentralized database is a modern-day storage solution that combines decentralized technologies with cutting-edge computing to randomly store data and files across multiple nodes, delivering high security and unmatched availability while being completely censorship-resistant.

title: Decentralized Derivatives
description: Decentralized derivatives merge the risk management and speculative opportunities of traditional derivatives with the principles of blockchain technology, creating a novel class of financial products.

title: Decentralized Exchange (DEX)
description: A peer-to-peer exchange allowing users to trade cryptocurrency without the need for an intermediary.

title: Decentralized Governance
description: Decentralized governance refers to the procedures through which a platform's disintermediated, equitable management is carried out for blockchain networks and dApps.

title: Decentralized GPU
description: Decentralized GPU infrastructure refers to a distributed network of graphical processing units (GPUs) that offers on-demand access to GPU computing power.

title: Decentralized Identifier (DID)
description: A decentralized identifier, or DID, refers to an ID that can be issued by an autonomous, independent, and decentralized platform that acts as a proof of ownership of digital identity.

title: Decentralized Marketplace
description: A decentralized marketplace, built on blockchain technology, allows traders or investors to trade with each other while eliminating middlemen. They are available globally and require no intermediaries to make trades possible.

title: Decentralized Network
description: A decentralized network is a collection of interconnected but distinct elements that interact with one another without the need for a centralized power or server.

title: Decentralized Order Book
description: A decentralized order book is a trading mechanism where buy and sell orders are matched through a distributed network of nodes, rather than being centralized in a single location or controlled by a single entity.

title: Decentralized Payment Network
description: A decentralized payment network refers to a system where users, customers, and vendors can exchange money without having to trust any third party to keep the network secure and operational.

title: Decentralized Social Media
description: Decentralized social media is a social media platform that is based on blockchain.

title: Decentralized Stablecoin
description: Decentralized stablecoins are fully transparent, non-custodial with no or partial third-party control.

title: Decentralized Vehicle-to-Everything (D-V2X)
description: D-V2X operates on a peer-to-peer network where nodes maintain copies of a decentralized ledger, ensuring data integrity and trust among participants in the V2X network.

title: Decryption
description: The process of transforming encrypted data back into a format that is readable by a user or machine.

title: DeFi
description: A movement encouraging alternatives to traditional, centralized forms of financial services. DeFi, or Decentralized Finance, refers to the growing ecosystem of applications and services that leverage blockchain tech and cryptocurrencies to provide decentralized financial services to end users.
