Typically deployed to the Ethereum network, hundreds of DeFi applications exist today that allow users to borrow, lend, and earn interest on cryptocurrencies. DeFi applications are made up of a collection of smart contracts that define the exact process and flow of user funds.

title: DeFi Aggregator
description: A DeFi aggregator brings together trades across various DeFi platforms into one place.

title: DeFi Degens
description: DeFi degenerates. A subculture associated with a disreputable corner of decentralized finance known for pump and dump schemes.

title: DeFi Mullet
description: A DeFi Mullet combines familiar fintech interfaces with decentralized backends, hiding blockchain complexity from users.

title: Deflation
description: A decline in the general level of prices for goods and services in an economy.

title: Delayed Proof of Work (dPoW)
description: Delayed Proof of Work (dPoW) is a second-layer consensus security mechanism, designed to protect blockchains from 51% attacks threatening the integrity of the network.

title: Delegated Proof-of-Stake (dPOS)
description: An alternative to the Proof-of-Stake and Proof-of-Work consensus algorithms.

title: Delisting
description: The process of removing an asset/stock/cryptocurrency from a trading exchange is called delisting.

title: Demurrage
description: Demurrage is a fee charged for using an asset beyond a certain time period.

title: Dencun Upgrade
description: Dencun is essentially the fusion of two separate upgrades - 'Deneb' and ‘Cancun’ together targeting improvements across Ethereum's consensus and execution layers.

title: Denial-of-Service (DoS) Attack
description: A denial-of-service attack aims to temporarily make a computer or network service unavailable to its intended users.

title: Depeg
description: A depeg is a situation in crypto markets whereby a crypto asset that is expected to maintain a predetermined market value in reference to another asset loses its ability to do so. This can be triggered by various technical and market-based factors and usually has ripple effects that negatively influence market dynamics.

title: DePEN
description: DePEN can be considered a vertical of DePIN, operating as a blockchain-powered ecosystem that facilitates the decentralized generation, distribution, and monetization of renewable energy.

title: DePIN
description: Decentralized Physical Infrastructure Network, or DePIN is an innovative approach that combines the power of blockchain with real-world physical infrastructure to create decentralized, efficient, and community-driven networks.

title: Depth Chart
description: A graph that plots the requests to buy (bids) and the requests to sell (asks) on a chart, based on limit orders. The chart shows the point at which the market is most likely to accept a transaction.

title: Derivative
description: A financial instrument deriving its value from the value of an underlying asset.

title: Derivatives Market
description: A public market for derivatives, instruments such as futures contracts or options, which are derived from other forms of cryptocurrency assets.

title: Desktop Wallet
description: A desktop wallet is a type of software wallet that is usually non-custodial.

title: Deterministic Wallet
description: A type of cryptocurrency wallet in which keys and addresses are created from a single seed.

title: Dex Aggregator
description: DEX aggregators are a relatively new type of blockchain-based service that allow cryptocurrency traders to benefit from a large variety of financial tools in a single interface, often providing better liquidity and prices on different crypto pairs.

title: Dharma Protocol
description: An open-source stack for building debt markets on Ethereum.

title: Diamond Hands
description: Diamond Hands is a popular term on social media platforms. It refers to people who hold their coins even if their portfolio drops in value by more than 20%.

title: Difficulty
description: A measure of how hard it is to validate a new block on a blockchain. The mining difficulty of a cryptocurrency is how difficult it is to find the right hash for the next block.

title: Digital
description: Digital technologies are these electronic tools that have the ability to generate, store, or even process data.

title: Digital Art
description: Digital art is art and media that is made by using digital technology.

title: Digital Asset
description: A digital asset is an asset securitized in a digital manner, e.g., by a token. A digital asset refers to the digital representation of something of value.

title: Digital Asset Custodian
description: A digital asset custodian is responsible for looking after digital assets on behalf of an investor or client.

title: Digital Asset Ecosystem
description: Digital asset ecosystem is a term that defines everything involved in the crypto space. From NFTs to futures, this term sums up all the facilities offered and elements associated with the crypto universe.

title: Digital Barter Economy
description: Eliminating the inherent weaknesses of the traditional barter economy, a digital barter economy makes it easier to trade both physical and virtual items anywhere in the world.

title: Digital Commodity
description: A commodity that exists digitally, as opposed to in 'meatspace.'

title: Digital Currency (Electronic Money, Digital Money)
description: A digital currency is a type of currency that is non-physical (i.e., no banknotes and coins exist thereof) and which can only be transmitted via electronic means, typically allowing for instantaneous transactions and borderless transfer of ownership. A currency that exists only in digital form, as opposed to traditional physical currencies. Fiat refers to types of money within the traditional financial ecosystem. Examples of fiat currencies are the US dollar and the euro. Working with fiat currencies in the blockchain landscape typically requires trusting a centralized central entity to custody your funds.

title: Digital Dollar
description: The term 'digital dollar' refers to a possible digital currency issued by the US central bank (CBDC).

title: Digital Identity
description: A digital identity is a set of information representing an external entity on a computer system. Information used by a person or entity to identify themselves to a computer or network.

title: Digital Money
description: See digital currency.

title: Digital Payment
description: A digital payment is a computer-based transaction of money authorized electronically on an electronic device.

title: Digital Security
description: A digital security is a digital representation of a security for which ownership is verified and recorded using DLT.

title: Digital Security Offering
description: See DSO.

title: Digital Signature
description: A method for proving the authenticity of a digital communication. See signature.

title: Digital Signature Algorithm (DSA)
description: Digital Signature Algorithm (DSA) is a signature algorithm, not an encryption algorithm, and uses public-key cryptography to generate digital signatures.

title: Digital Token
description: See token.

title: Digital Wallet (Mobile Wallet, mWallet)
description: A digital wallet is an electronic device that stores payment and authentication information and thus permits an individual to make electronic payments and/or mobile payments. By using digital wallets, users can purchase items online with a computer or use smartphones to purchase something at a store. Some digital wallets also permit money transfers among users. Wallets are tools used by crypto holders to control their private keys and provide an interface to make transactions. Both software and hardware wallets are available today, and wallets can be custodial or non-custodial. The distinction here is whether or not the user retains full control of their private key.

title: Dildo
description: No, not that. The red or green 'candles,' or vertical lines, on graphs showing cryptocurrency market data.

title: Dip
description: A dip is when markets experience a short or protracted downturn.

title: Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG)
description: A DAG is a directed graph such that starting at any node and following the vertices along their direction, there is no way to return to the original starting node; DAGs are being used as an alternative DLT to blockchain technology. A way of structuring data, often used for data modelling, and increasingly as a consensus tool in cryptocurrencies.

title: Discord
description: Discord is a web-based communication tool or application primarily built to enable communication between gamers.

title: Distributed Consensus
description: Collective agreement reached among nodes in a network.

title: Distributed Denial of Service (DDoS) Attack
description: An attempt by a bad actor to disrupt the operation of an application, server, or network by flooding it with traffic.

title: Distributed Ledger
description: A distributed ledger is a digital system recording and storing data and which is consensually shared and synchronized across a geographically spread network across multiple sites, institutions, and/or geographies. Distributed ledgers are ledgers in which data is stored across a network of decentralized nodes. A distributed ledger does not necessarily involve a cryptocurrency and may be permissioned and private.

title: Distributed Ledger Technology (DLT)
description: A database that is shared by multiple participants, in multiple places. The basis for blockchains. DLT is a technology based on the principle of distributed ledgers.

title: Distributed Network
description: A network in which the data and applications are dependent on multiple sources, as opposed to one location.

title: Distributed Validator Technology (DVT)
description: DVT operates as a security system for staking validators. It spreads key management and signs tasks across multiple parties.

title: Distribution Phase
description: The distribution phase is the opposite of the accumulation phase. In this phase, the market moves sideways and is range-bound after experiencing an extended uptrend.

title: Diversification
description: Diversification is a risk-management strategy that mixes a wide variety of investments within a portfolio.

title: Diversified Proof of Stake
description: Diversified Proof of Stake is a variation of the popular PoS consensus mechanism that allows multiple assets to be staked on a single blockchain.

title: Documentation
description: Documentation is a part of token economies that stores all the details of an asset on the blockchain.

title: Dolphin
description: Someone with a moderate holding of cryptocurrency.

title: Dominance
description: A measure of Bitcoin's value in the context of the larger cryptocurrency market. Bitcoin Dominance is a metric that determines how much share of the overall crypto market share is owned by Bitcoin.

title: Dorian Nakamoto
description: Dorian Nakamoto is a Japanese-American physicist who some believe to be Satoshi Nakamoto.

title: DotSama
description: DotSama is a new piece of crypto slang, used to describe the Kusama and Polkadot ecosystems in just one word.

title: Double Spend Attack
description: A double-spend attack is a practice in the world of digital currencies where a user gains the ability to spend the same cryptocurrency more than once. A double-spend attack is a practice in the world of digital currencies where a user gains the ability to spend the same cryptocurrency more than once.

title: Double Spending
description: The potential for a digital currency to be spent twice. Double spending is a process that exploits a flaw in a cryptocurrency system which allows a user to spend a single coin twice.

title: dPoSec (Distributed Proof of Security)
description: dPoSec (Distributed Proof of Security) is a consensus mechanism designed to ensure that the blockchain network continues to operate even if a third of the nodes are compromised. It addresses the key challenges faced by the existing distributed network of nodes and validators.

title: Drawdown
description: The maximum reduction in value from the peak value for an investment or fund that has occurred over a period of time.

title: DRC-20
description: DRC-20 is a token standard on the Dogecoin network that allows developers and users to create fungible assets within Dogecoin's ecosystem. It is similar to ERC-20 on Ethereum.

title: Drivechain
description: Drivechain is a Bitcoin improvement proposal that aims to scale Bitcoin and add new features using sidechains.

title: Dual Governance
description: Dual governance refers to a two-pronged system of decision-making in a decentralized autonomous organization (DAO), where two distinct parties are involved in running the organization rather than one larger body.

title: Dual-Token Economy/Model (Two-Token Economy)
description: In the world of blockchain, a dual-token economy or model means a project with two tokens, one of which is used for utility inside the network and the other one as security to raise funds for the crypto project.

title: Dump
description: A sudden sell-off of digital assets.

title: Dumping
description: A collective market sell-off that occurs when large quantities of a particular cryptocurrency are sold in a short period of time.

title: Dust Transactions
description: Miniscule amounts of Bitcoin in a wallet — with a value that would be outweighed by the cost of a transaction fee.

title: Dusting Attack
description: An attack that aims to uncover the identity of a wallet’s owner, information that can subsequently be used in phishing scams.

title: DYCO (Dynamic Coin Offering)
description: DYCO (dynamic coin offering) is a new crowdfunding model developed by DAO Maker that employs utility tokens that are backed by USD.

title: DYOR
description: The acronym of Do Your Own Research — encouraging investors to complete due diligence into a project before investing. Do Your Own Research. This is often used as a warning by cryptocurrency influencers to other investors, reminding us that there is no better substitute for due diligence than our own research. It is worth remembering that cryptocurrency influencers and media outlets can have different incentives and goals. Ask yourself; is the news article or review I am reading unbiased, and factually correct?

title: E-Signature
description: An electronic signature, or e-signature, is any electronic mark (sign, sound, symbol, etc.) used in place of a physical signature in signing a document or contract.

title: Economic Utility
description: Economic utility is a term in economics that refers to the total satisfaction that a person can derive from consuming a good or service.

title: Edge Nodes
description: In computer science, an edge node is a computer that serves as an end-user gateway to form a connection with other nodes.

title: Effective Proof-of-Stake
description: Effective Proof-of-Stake is Harmony’s version of the Proof-of-Stake consensus mechanism that aims for both security and decentralization.

title: EIP-1559
description: EIP-1559 is an upgrade to the Ethereum network that simplified the fee market mechanism.

title: Electrum Wallet
description: A Bitcoin wallet for Windows, macOS, and Linux with a simple interface.

title: ELI5
description: Short for 'explain like I’m five' — a plea for simplicity when crypto concepts are being explained.

title: Elliott Waves
description: The Elliott Wave Theory is an essential tool for many stock and crypto market traders.

title: EMA (Exponential Moving Average)
description: Exponential moving average (EMA) is a technical indicator that highlights the recent price changes and data points of an asset/stock/cryptocurrency while keeping the older chart observations intact.

title: Email Spoofing
description: Email spoofing is a technique that is used to trick users into thinking that a message actually came from a different person.

title: Emission
description: The speed at which new coins are produced and released.

title: Encryption
description: Encryption is a method through which information can be made into code.

title: Enterprise Blockchain
description: Enterprise blockchain is the use of distributed ledger technology for non-speculative business purposes. Tailored for the needs of enterprises, these chains may be private or public.

title: Enterprise Ethereum Alliance (EEA)
description: A group of organizations and companies working together to further develop the Ethereum network. The EEA is an alliance of organizations developing standards allowing for harmonization and standardization of Ethereum applications.

title: Epoch
description: One entire run of the training dataset through the algorithm is referred to as an epoch in machine learning.

title: Equity
description: Equity is the funds that would be returned to a company's shareholders if all of the company's assets were dissolved and all debts were paid off in the event of liquidation.

title: Erasure Coding
description: Erasure coding is a method of storing data at multiple locations after doing its segmentation, expansion, and encoding with redundant information.

title: ERC 7512
description: ERC-7512 aims to standardize how audit reports are represented directly on Ethereum's blockchain.

title: ERC-1155
description: ERC-1155 digital token standard was created by Enjin and offers more security in comparison to older token standards. It can be used to create both fungible and non-fungible assets on the Ethereum network.

title: ERC-20
description: Tokens designed and used solely on the Ethereum platform. ERC-20 is a quasi-standard comprising a list of rules set forth for all Ethereum tokens, so that developers can accurately predict how new tokens will function within the Ethereum system. Used on the Ethereum network, ERC-20 is the most vastly used crypto-token standard. It allows developers to easily create digital currencies, which are immediately compatible with existing infrastructure.

title: ERC-223
description: ERC-223 is an Ethereum token standard that is powered by smart contracts that enable users to securely transfer tokens to a digital wallet.

title: ERC-721
description: A token standard for non-fungible Ethereum tokens. ERC-721 is a quasi-standard for non-fungible tokens comprising a list of rules set forth for non-fungible Ethereum tokens, so that developers can accurately predict how tokens will function within the Ethereum system that bear unique information. This is the Ethereum network’s standard for non-fungible tokens—NFTs. It allows for creation of unique, uncounterfitable tokens. It can be used for creating digital collectibles and gaming items or to tokenize real-world unique items.

title: ERC-7683
description: A universal standard that allows web3 apps to express complex multi-step cross-chain transactions as a single user request.

title: ERC-777
description: ERC-777 is a tradable token standard spun out from ERC-20 to enable a new way to engage with a token contract while staying backward compatible.

title: ERC-827
description: ERC-827 is an ETH token standard that addresses the existing limitations of ERC 20 when it comes to the implementation of calls in transfers and approvals in particular.

title: ERC-884
description: ERC-884 facilitates the creation of tradable ERC-20 tokens, each of which symbolizes a numberless share issued by a Delaware corporation.

title: ERC-948
description: ERC-948 is a new Ethereum token protocol that is designed to connect subscription businesses with customers and allows for subscription-based transactions.

title: Escrow
description: A financial instrument where assets or cash are held by a third party while a buyer and a seller complete a deal.

title: Esports
description: Electronic sports, commonly known as e-sports, is a term used for digital gaming competition, in which players battle against each other in an individual or team-based format often in a competition or event that offers huge monetary rewards to the winners.

title: ETH/BTC
description: ETH/BTC is a popular cryptocurrency trading pair that denominates the price of Ethereum in Bitcoin.

title: Ethash
description: Ethash is the algorithm utilized for the proof of work mining Ethereum and ETH-based cryptocurrencies.

title: Ether
description: The form of payment used in the operation of the distribution application platform, Ethereum. Ether is a cryptocurrency token which can be transferred between accounts and used to compensate Ethereum nodes for computations performed.

title: Ethereum
description: Ethereum is a public blockchain-based decentralized computing platform for applications that run smart contracts.

title: Ethereum Difficulty
description: Ethereum difficulty plays a crucial role in maintaining the stability and security of the network, especially in the context of Ethereum's transition to a Proof-of-Stake (PoS) consensus mechanism.

title: Ethereum ETF
description: An Ethereum Exchange Traded Fund (ETF) is an investment tool that allows investors to get exposed to Ethereum's price movements through traditional stock exchanges.

title: Ethereum Improvement Proposal (EIP)
description: Ethereum Improvement Proposals (EIPs) describe standards for the Ethereum platform, including core protocol specifications, client APIs, and contract standards.

title: Ethereum Request For Comment (ERC)
description: Ethereum Request for Comment (ERC) is the protocol to introduce new improvements to the network by developers.

title: Ethereum Transaction
description: Ethereum transactions are cryptographically signed instructions to initiate a transaction to update the state of the Ethereum network.

title: Ethereum Virtual Machine (EVM)
description: A Turing-complete virtual machine that enables execution of code exactly as intended; it is the runtime environment for every smart contract. Every Ethereum node runs on the EVM to maintain consensus across the blockchain. The Ethereum Virtual Machine, or EVM, is essentially a global blockchain-based computer. It provides a runtime environment for developers to create trustless, decentralized applications on the Ethereum network.

title: Event Triggers
description: When a transaction is mined, smart contracts can emit events and write logs to the blockchain which the frontend can then process.

title: Exchange
description: Businesses that allow customers to trade cryptocurrencies for fiat money or other cryptocurrencies.

title: Exchange Traded Fund (ETF)
description: A security that tracks a basket of assets such as stocks, bonds, and cryptocurrencies but can be traded like a single stock.

title: Exit Scam
description: An exit scam is a trick where projects disappear (or shut down) after accumulating investors’ money. In other industries, this event occurs when a business stops shipping orders even if it is receiving payment for the new ones.

title: Fair AI
description: Fair AI is a new approach to artificial intelligence (AI) that has an emphasis on decentralization and equitability through rewarding individuals for their data and compute contributions.

title: Fakeout
description: A fakeout is a sudden and temporary move in a market that tricks traders into thinking that a trend is emerging when, in fact, it is not.

title: Falling Knife
description: A falling knife refers to the price dive of an asset and denotes a downward momentum of the financial market.

title: Falling Wedge
description: Falling wedges, also known as descending wedges, have a distinct downward slope and a bullish bias in comparison to symmetrical triangles, which have no discernible slope and no bias.

title: Fan Token
description: A fan token is a cryptocurrency issued by a specific sports team and allows its holders to participate in the governing activities and attain exclusive rewards and discounts.

title: FATF Travel Rule
description: The FATF Travel Rule requires virtual asset service providers to regulate information sharing for certain large transactions.

title: Faucet
description: A cryptocurrency reward system usually on a website or app, that rewards users for completing certain tasks.

title: Fee Tiers
description: Fee tiers refer to the fee structure that determines the amount charged when investors deposit or withdraw money and execute trades on a crypto exchange.

title: Fiat
description: Fiat currency is 'legal tender' backed by a central government, such as the Federal Reserve, with its own banking system, such as fractional reserve banking. It can take the form of physical cash, or it can be represented electronically, such as with bank credit.

title: Fiat On-Ramp
description: A fiat-on ramp is a way to get cryptocurrency from fiat, or regular money.

title: Fiat-Pegged Cryptocurrency
description: A coin, token, or asset issued on a blockchain that is linked to a government or bank-issued currency.

title: Fibonacci Retracement Level
description: The Fibonacci retracement method uses a set of key numbers called Fibonacci ratios to identify the support and resistance levels of an asset/stock/cryptocurrency.

title: Field Programmable Gate Array
description: Field Programmable Gate Array is an integrated circuit that allows customers or designers to reconfigure as per requirement after the manufacturing process.

title: Financial Action Task Force (FATF)
description: The FATF is a global organization that sets global standards to combat money laundering and terrorist financing (AML/CFT).

title: Financial Crimes Enforcement Network (FinCEN)
description: The Financial Crimes Enforcement Network (FinCEN) is a federal regulatory bureau of the United States Treasury.

title: Financial Transactions and Reports Analysis Centre (FINTRAC)
description: The Financial Transactions and Reports Analysis Centre (FINTRAC) of Canada is the nation’s financial intelligence agency.

title: First In, First Out
description: First in, First Out (FIFO) is an inventory method used to specify your cost-basis when calculating your taxes.

title: First-Mover Advantage (FMA)
description: The first-mover advantage refers to the launch of an innovative product or service which provides a head-start to a company by creating brand loyalty and penetrating markets before their future competitors. A first-mover advantage is the advantage a business gains by being the first significant player to enter a new market or market segment, leading to higher revenues and profits over time.

title: Fish
description: Someone who has a small crypto investment.

title: Flash Crash
description: A flash crash is a market condition where an asset’s price falls very rapidly within a very brief time interval.

title: Flash Loan
description: A flash loan is a transaction in which a specific quantity of liquidity is borrowed and repaid in the same transaction or block.

title: Flash Loan Attack
description: Flash loan attacks are when malicious actors exploit a smart contract.

title: Flash Loans
description: Flash loans are a type of uncollateralized lending used in decentralized finance (DeFi).

title: Flashbots
description: Flashbots was launched as an independent research and development organization with an aim to lessen the adverse effects of the Maximal Extractable Value (MEV) extraction.

title: Flatcoin
description: Flatcoins are cryptocurrencies whose value is pegged to the cost of living, rather than fiat or commodity.

title: Flippening
description: A hypothetical scenario where Ethereum's market cap overtakes Bitcoin's. The Flippening is the name of a potential future event when Bitcoin is overtaken by another cryptocurrency in terms of market capitalization.

title: Flipping
description: An investment strategy where you buy something with the goal of reselling for a profit later, usually in a short period of time.

title: FOMO
description: FOMO is an acronym for 'Fear Of Missing Out' and describes the anxiety that a value-creating event may currently be happening and that one misses out on it; it regularly describes the buying behavior when cryptocurrencies are suddenly gaining in value and more buyers appear to enter the market all of a sudden. Fear of missing out. A type of social anxiety arising from the notion that others are having fun or enjoying the benefits of an event while the person experiencing FOMO isn’t. In crypto markets, it usually refers to watching the tokens you do not own go through explosive upward price movements.

title: Fork (Blockchain)
description: Forks, or chain splits, create an alternate version of the blockchain, leaving two blockchains to run simultaneously. A fork is a split in a blockchain resulting in two individual blockchains; forks can be caused by 51% attack, errors in the program code, or a new set of rules for the consensus process. A fork describes a situation where a blockchain experiences a change in the protocol that produces two parallel chains. Forks typically occur when crypto developers or communities decide that the protocol must be changed or updated in some way. There are two types of forks: hard forks, which break backwards-compatibility and cause a new currency to be issued, and soft forks, which update the ruleset—requiring support from a majority of the network’s participants.

title: Fork (Software)
description: When an entirely new program has been developed from source code, taken from an open-source software.

title: Fork Choice Rule
description: The fork choice rule is a mechanism in Ethereum that allows nodes to agree on a canonical chain when the network splits into competing forks.

title: Fractional Stablecoins
description: A fractional stablecoin is one that is backed in two ways: collaterally-backed and algorithmically modified.

title: Fraud Proof
description: A fraud proof is a technological method that functions as a bond in a decentralized environment that uses Optimistic Rollups (ORs), which are sidechains that aim to reduce the costs and latency that dApps might encounter on a blockchain platform.

title: Front Running
description: Front running is when you place a transaction in a queue when you have knowledge of a future transaction.

title: Fundamental Analysis (FA)
description: Fundamental analysis is a method of evaluating the underlying economic and financial factors that affect the value of an asset. A method of evaluating the value of a given asset, as well as estimating its future performance. In the context of crypto projects, it takes into account such factors as the technological and innovative value of a product, the team behind it, or the token distribution model and its use cases.

title: Funding Payments
description: Funding payments are periodic payments between traders. These are designed to reduce the discrepancy between the perpetual market price and the spot market price.

title: Fungible
description: In cryptocurrency, fungibility is when a coin or token can be replaced by any other identical coin or token. Fungible is a quality of an asset denoting that the asset can be exchanged for another asset of a similar or identical type without any significant loss occurring to the holder; to be fungible, tokens must not bear any unique information.

title: Fusion Rollups
description: Fusion rollups are a blockchain scalability solution that combines the best of other L2 approaches such as Appchains and Shared Rollups.

title: Futo
description: Futo is an organization that develops and invests in decentralized technologies and companies.

title: Futures
description: A futures contract is a standardized legal agreement to buy or sell a particular commodity or asset at a predetermined price at a specified time in the future.

title: Gas
description: A term used on the Ethereum platform that refers to a unit of measuring the computational effort of conducting transactions or smart contracts, or launch DApps in the Ethereum network. It is the 'fuel' of the Ethereum network. Gas is a measurement of how much processing is required by the Ethereum network to process a transaction; transactions with higher Gas prices are prioritized by the network. Refers to a fee charged for performing operations on Ethereum network—sending transactions and deploying and interacting with smart contracts. It is usually priced in Gwei, a small fraction of Ether.

title: Gas Limit
description: A term used on the Ethereum platform that refers to the maximum amount of gas the user is willing to spend on a transaction. The Gas limit is the maximum amount of Gas a user is willing to spend for a particular transaction on the Ethereum network.

title: Gas Price
description: A term used on the Ethereum platform that refers to the price you are willing to pay for a transaction. Gas price is the price of a Gas unit denominated in Ether.

title: Gas Station Networks (GSN)
description: Gas Station Networks (GSN) allows you to build decentralized applications (dApps) which provide payment for transactions, so users do not need to hold Ether or ETH to pay for gas, easing their onboarding process and improving user acquisition and experience.

title: Gavin Wood
description: Gavin Wood is the co-founder of Parity Technologies, and one of the founders of Ethereum.

title: Gems
description: Gem is a term for relatively unknown low-cap coins that have immense potential or are grossly undervalued.

title: Genesis Block
description: The first block of data that is processed and validated to form a new blockchain, often referred to as block 0 or block 1. The genesis block is the very first block of any blockchain.

title: Geotagged NFT
description: Geotagged non-fungible tokens (NFT) feature 3D versions of the street art alongside the corresponding geo-location. They allow art aficionados to own both the virtual and physical artwork without the need to remove the actual infrastructure it was originally painted on.

title: Geth
description: Geth, short for Go Ethereum, is a command-line interface that allows developers to run full Ethereum nodes, mine the cryptocurrency, and execute smart contracts.

title: GigaChad in Crypto
description: In crypto, GigaChad refers to someone who consistently makes smart investment moves and maintains unwavering confidence in their trading strategy, especially during market downturns.

title: GitHub
description: GitHub is one of the most popular code hosting platforms, allowing developers to collaborate on various projects. GitHub is an online hosting service for version control using Git; GitHub is Web-based and offers distributed version control and source code management functionality.

title: Goguen Phase
description: The Goguen phase of Cardano allows the development of smart contracts and DApps.

title: Gold-Backed Cryptocurrency
description: A coin or token issued that represents a value of gold; for example, one physical gram of gold equals one coin.

title: Golden Cross
description: A golden cross is a bullish technical trading indicator that emerges when the 50-day moving average crosses the 200-day moving average, signaling an imminent price rise of the asset/stock/cryptocurrency.

title: Google Authenticator
description: Google Authenticator is a software-based verification system that generates unique one-time codes that are time-based on your mobile phone.

title: Governance
description: In the world of cryptocurrencies, governance is defined as the people or organizations that have decision-making powers regarding the project.

title: Governance Token
description: A governance token is a token that can be used to vote on decisions that influence an ecosystem.

title: GPG Encryption
description: GNU Privacy Guard (GPG) is a popular OpenPGP (Pretty Good Privacy) implementation. It is an open-source alternative to PGP that encrypts and signs communications and data securely.

title: Graphical Processing Unit (GPU)
description: More commonly known as a graphics card, it is a computer chip that creates 3D images on computers, but has turned out to be efficient for mining cryptocurrencies.

title: Gray Swan Event
description: Gray swan is a significant event whose occurrence could be predicted, but the likelihood is low.

title: Greater Fool Theory
description: The greater fool theory was first discussed by professor Burton Malkiel. It suggests that there is always a 'greater fool' that you can sell an overvalued asset to.

title: Green Candle
description: A green candle is an indication of the price closing higher than the opening price. The green candle is an indication that the overall sentiment of the market at the time of trading was bullish, or positive. A wide body with a small tail on top indicates a strong bullish movement in the market.

title: Group Mining
description: As opposed to solo mining, group mining is when multiple people mine together.

title: Gwei
description: The denomination used in defining the cost of gas in transactions involving Ether. Gwei is a denomination of Ether and a popular measurement unit of Gas. 1 Ether = 1,000,000,000 Gwei. Wei is the smallest, indivisible part of Ethereum’s native currency Ether. 1 Ether is equal to 1,000,000,000,000,000,000 wei (1018). Gwei stands for Giga-wei, so 109 wei.

title: Hacking
description: Hacking is the process of using a computer to manipulate another computer or computer system in an unauthorized fashion.

title: Hal Finney
description: Hal Finney was a cryptographer and programmer who pioneered Bitcoin’s development and worked with Satoshi Nakamoto.

title: Hard Cap
description: The hard cap is the maximum number of coins that will ever be created (e.g., 21 million for Bitcoin); not all cryptocurrencies have a hard cap (e.g., Ether). A hard cap is the absolute maximum supply of a digital asset.

title: Hard Fork (Blockchain)
description: A type of protocol change that validates all previously invalid transactions, and invalidates all previously valid transactions. A hard fork is a specific form of a fork which occurs when the developers of a blockchain decide that changes must be made to the code so that it will create lasting incompatibilities between the older and newer version; contrary to a soft fork, a hard fork requires that all nodes upgrade to the new version of the code; as all nodes will only recognize the new blocks as valid, a hard fork is backward-incompatible.

title: Hard Fork Combinator
description: A hard fork combinator (first designed by IOHK) is a tool to combine protocols specifically on the Cardano blockchain after a hard fork has occurred.

title: Hard Peg
description: A hard peg is an exchange rate policy, where a currency is set at a fixed rate against another currency.

title: Hardware Security Module
description: A hardware security module is a type of computing device that secures digital keys and encrypts data.

title: Hardware Wallet
description: A hardware wallet is a wallet for cryptocurrencies that usually resembles a USB stick. See cold wallet.

title: Hash
description: A hash is the result of executing a hash function. A hash is the output result of a hashing algorithm, which creates a unique, fixed-length string to encrypt and secure a certain selection of arbitrary data.

title: Hash Code
description: See hash.

title: Hash Function
description: A hash function is a one-way algorithm used to map data of arbitrary size onto data of a fixed size. Any function used to map data of arbitrary size to data of a fixed size. SHA-256 is a one-way cryptographic hashing algorithm that takes an input of any size and converts it to a random string of a fixed length. Many blockchain projects today make use of SHA-256 to enable proof-of-work mining for verifying transactions.

title: Hash Rate
description: Measuring unit of processing power—it tells how many network-specific calculations are done per second by the Bitcoin network. A hash rate of 1 tera hash means the network can perform 1 trillion calculations in one second. Hash Power / Hash Rate is a unit of measurement for the amount of computing power being consumed by the network to continuously operate.

title: Hash Value
description: See hash.

title: Hashed
description: Hashed is an attribute of a data set which has been transformed using a hash function.

title: Hashgraph
description: Hashgraph is a DLT and alternative to blockchain which achieves superior performance by using a consensus mechanism based on a virtual voting algorithm combined with the gossip protocol.

title: Hashgraph Consensus Mechanism
description: The hashgraph consensus is an advanced and up-to-date version of the technology that enables consensus mechanisms.

title: Hashing
description: Hashing describes the process of executing a hash function.

title: Hashrate
description: The hashrate denotes the speed at which hash functions are executed.

title: Hashed Timelock Contract (HTLC)
description: A hashed timelock contract (HTLC) is an agreement between two parties that requires no trust between two users by offering special features to reduce risk.

title: Haskell Programming Language
description: The Haskell programming language is a standardized, general-purpose, statically-typed, purely functional programming language that came into existence in 1990.

title: Hedge Contract
description: A hedge contract is a form of insurance that investors use to hedge against the risk of financial loss. Typically, a hedge is designed to protect against price fluctuations in the market.

title: Hedge Fund
description: A hedge fund is pooled investment fund that employs a number of investment strategies in a variety of liquid asset classes.

title: Hidden Cap
description: Hidden cap is an unknown limit to the amount of money a team elects to receive from investors in its initial coin offering (ICO). The purpose of a hidden cap is to even the playing field by letting smaller investors put in money, without the large investors forming an accurate understanding of the total cap and adjusting their investment as a result.

title: Hierarchical Deterministic Wallet (HD Wallet)
description: A wallet that uses Hierarchical Deterministic (HD) protocol to support the generation of crypto-wallets from a single master seed using 12 mnemonic phrases.

title: Higher High
description: A higher high is when the price of a cryptocurrency closes higher than the previous day, which itself closed at a high.

title: Higher Low
description: A higher low is when the price of a cryptocurrency closes at a level that is higher than the close of the previous day.

title: HODL
description: A type of passive investment strategy where you hold an investment for a long period of time, regardless of any changes in the price or markets. The term first became famous due to a typo made in a Bitcoin forum, and the term is now commonly expanded to stand for 'Hold On for Dear Life.' The term comes from misspelling the word hold. It refers to the action of not selling your cryptos, often in opposition to the current market trend.

title: Honeyminer
description: Honeyminer is a cryptocurrency mining app available for download on multiple devices.

title: Honeypot
description: A honeypot is a scam used in the crypto industry to trap victims and steal their assets or sensitive information.

title: Hostage Byte Attack
description: A Hostage Byte Attack is a distributed denial of service (DDoS) attack against a user that stored its data on a malicious storage node and is asked to pay ransom to retrieve its data.

title: Hosted Wallet
description: A wallet managed by a third-party service.

title: Hot Storage
description: The online storage of private keys allowing for quicker access to cryptocurrencies. Hot storage is an electronic device that stores cryptocurrencies and which is connected to the Internet; it is typically easier to set up, access, and scale than cold storage but more susceptible to unauthorized access, hacking attacks, and other vulnerabilities.

title: Hot Wallet
description: A cryptocurrency wallet that is connected to the internet for hot storage of cryptoassets. A hot wallet is a digital wallet employing hot storage technology.

title: Howey Test
description: A test used to determine whether or not an asset is a security.

title: Human Keys
description: Human Keys are cryptographic keys derived from what you are, what you know, or what you have. They are used to secure digital assets, protect privacy, and access the decentralized web.

title: Human-Readable Names
description: In computer programming, human-readable refers to making information able to be read naturally by humans and stands in opposition to machine-readable format - i.e., binary.

title: Huobi BTC (HBTC)
description: Launched by Huobi, Huobi BTC or HBTC is a standard ERC-20 token pegged to BTC on a 1:1 ratio.

title: Hybrid PoW/PoS
description: A hybrid PoW/PoS allows for both proof-of-stake and proof-of-work as consensus distribution algorithms on the network. This approach aims to bring together the security of PoW consensus and the governance and energy efficiency of PoS.

title: Hydra (Cardano)
description: Hydra is a layer-two scaling solution for the Cardano blockchain that aims to increase the transaction processing capacity of the network by allowing multiple heads or channels.

title: Hyperinflation
description: Hyperinflation is defined as unrestricted growth in prices for goods and services in an economy. It happens when resources become limited, such as gas or food, and prices rise as demand outstrips supply.

title: Hyperledger (Hyperledger Foundation)
description: Hyperledger is an umbrella project of open-source blockchains and blockchain-related tools started by the Linux Foundation in 2015 to support the collaborative development of blockchain-based distributed ledgers.

title: Immutable
description: A property that defines the inability to be changed, especially over time. Immutability is a feature of data stored on the blockchain. Hence, the blockchain contains a history of transactions which is typically permanent, inerasable, and unalterable. Immutability can be threatened by extraordinary events, such as 51% attacks.

title: Impermanent Loss
description: Impermanent loss is when a liquidity provider has a temporary loss of funds because of volatility in a trading pair.

title: In-the-Money / Out-of-the-Money
description: In-the-money and out-of-the-money are options trading mechanisms that allow investors to benefit from additional tools to work with the market.

title: Infinite Approval
description: Pre-approving smart contracts to enable the platform to spend any amount of your coins.

title: Infinite Mint Attack
description: An infinite mint attack occurs when an unwanted entity or hacker mints an absurd ('infinite') amount of tokens within a protocol.

title: Inflation
description: A general increase in prices and fall in the purchasing value of money.

title: Initial Bounty Offering (IBO)
description: A novel way of launching a project that focuses on people contributing skills to a platform rather than money.

title: Initial Coin Offering (ICO)
description: Short for Initial Coin Offering, an ICO is a type of crowdfunding, or crowdsale, using cryptocurrencies as a means of raising capital for early-stage companies. An initial coin offering is an unregulated means of crowdfunding applied by cryptocurrency businesses as an alternative to the rigorous and regulated capital-raising process required by venture capitalists, banks, or stock exchanges. In an ICO, a percentage of the newly issued cryptocurrency is sold to investors in exchange for legal tender or other cryptocurrencies such as Bitcoin.

title: Initial Dex Offering (IDO)
description: An initial dex offering (IDX) is an alternative to an initial coin offering (ICO).

title: Initial Exchange Offering
description: A type of crowdfunding where crypto start-ups generate capital by listing through an exchange.

title: Initial Farm Offering (IFO)
description: Initial Farm Offering (IFO) helps DeFi projects raise capital through the farming feature offered by decentralized exchanges.

title: Initial Game Offering (IGO)
description: Initial game offerings (IGOs) provide individuals with an opportunity to invest in gaming projects at an early stage that have the potential to offer huge returns after their launch.

title: Initial NFT Offering (INO)
description: A crypto crowdfunding solution where projects can raise funds by listing a set of NFTs via a launchpad.

title: Initial Public Offering (IPO)
description: An initial public offering (IPO) is the process of a company offering shares for purchase on the stock market for the first time. An initial public offering is the process by which a private company is offered for the first time to the general public on a securities exchange. IPOs are typically conducted by small and young firms seeking capital to expand, but they can also be done by large privately-owned companies looking to become publicly traded.

title: Initial Stake Pool Offering (ISPO)
description: The Initial Stake Pool Offering (ISPO) is a comparatively new crypto fundraising method exclusive to the Cardano ecosystem. Unlike existing fundraising models, ISPOs are more inclusive, decentralized, equitable, and secure.

title: Initial Token Offering (ITO)
description: ITOs are similar to initial coin offerings — but have more of a focus on offering tokens with intrinsic utility in the form of software or usage in an ecosystem. An Initial Token Offering is largely identical to an Initial Coin Offering, yet it typically pertains to projects built on the Ethereum blockchain.

title: Input-Output Hong Kong (IOHK)
description: Now Input-Output Global, Input-Output Hong Kong (IOHK) was launched in 2015 by Charles Hoskinson to provide a blockchain infrastructure to research and engineering companies.

title: Insider Trading
description: Insider trading happens when someone purchases or sells stocks while possessing private, material information about that stock.

title: Instamine
description: When a large portion of a coin’s total supply is distributed to investors shortly after launch.

title: Instant Settlement Network Layer
description: Instant settlement network allows participants to exchange digital assets in real-time from anywhere in the world.

title: Institutional Investor
description: An Institutional Investor is an organization or a legal entity that trades in the market on behalf of its clients that may be retail investors.

title: Insurance Fund
description: An exchange insurance fund is used to cover any unexpected losses from leveraged trading. This fund is used to prevent traders from bankruptcy in the event of liquidations.

title: Integrated Application
description: Integrated Applications combine the advantages of appchains (customizability, performance, interoperability) and smart contracts (composability, standardization, minimal overhead) to enable developers to build better applications that can onboard users from anywhere.

title: Integrated Development Environment (IDE)
description: An integrated development environment (IDE) is a type of software that helps you develop apps by merging many development tools into a single graphical user interface (GUI).

title: Intellectual Property (IP)
description: Intellectual property (IP) is a type of property that can be legally protected from being copied or sold – it includes intangible creations that result from human thinking such as a book, song, design, business method, or software.

title: Intent
description: An intent refers to a user’s desired endstate or goal within an ecosystem.

title: Inter-Blockchain Communication (IBC)
description: Inter-Blockchain Communication (IBC) is a communication protocol that allows different blockchains to relay messages to each other.

title: Intercontinental Exchange (ICE)
description: The Intercontinental Exchange (ICE) is an American company founded in 2000 to purchase and operate global exchanges and clearing houses.

title: Interest Rates
description: A time-dependent charge or return made in proportion to the amount of money deposited, borrowed, or lent.

title: Intermediary/Middleman
description: A person or entity that acts as the go-between different parties to bring about agreements or carry out directives.

title: Internal Transaction
description: An internal transaction, also known as a 'message,' is a byproduct of an EOA interaction with a contract address that results in Ether being transferred.

title: Internet Layer
description: Internet layer is the third layer in the TCP/IP model and is responsible for the transportation of network packets.

title: Internet Memes
description: A meme is an image, a video, or a piece of text that is copied and spread rapidly by internet users. They are typically humorous but can also be critical as well.

title: Internet of Things
description: Internet of Things (IoT) is a global interconnected network of devices, sensors, and software that can collect and exchange data with each other in real-time over the Internet.

title: Internet Service Provider (ISP)
description: Internet Service Providers (ISPs) are commercial entities that provide end-users with access to the internet.

title: Interoperability
description: Blockchain interoperability, or cross-chain interoperability, is the ability to see and share information across multiple blockchains.

title: InterPlanetary File System (IPFS)
description: The InterPlatery File System is a peer-to-peer, distributed system for storing and accessing files, as well as websites and applications, which relies on content addressing rather than location.

title: Intrinsic Value
description: The intrinsic value of an asset depicts the asset’s actual worth based on a complex financial calculation rather than its current price.

title: Invest
description: Investing is when you put money in a financial scheme with the intent of making a gain.

title: Investment Vehicles (Crypto-tied)
description: Investment vehicles are the assets (classes) in which investors put their money with hopes to increase the value of their portfolio in the future.

title: IOU
description: An IOU, acronym for 'I owe you,' is a document stating that one party owes a debt to another party.

title: IP Address
description: IP addresses are unique numeric addresses assigned to devices connected to the internet or a local network.

title: Isolated Margin
description: This mode is useful for traders who want to take speculative positions, and where there is a probable chance of their speculation turning out to be incorrect. In such a situation, the user will be better protected as compared to cross-margin mode because only their isolated margin balance will face liquidation, instead of their entire margin balance. The downside of using isolated margin is that your exposure will be limited to one position in a particular market.

title: Jager
description: The smallest denomination of Binance Coin (BNB) is called Jager.

title: Java
description: Java is a general-purpose, class-based as well as object-oriented programming language.

title: JavaScript
description: JavaScript is a powerful, dynamic, lightweight, and advanced programming language. It is mostly used in web-based applications.

title: JOMO
description: Joy of missing out (JOMO) is the opposite of having a fear of missing out (FOMO).

title: Keylogger
description: A keylogger or keystroke logging software is a spying tool often used by hackers to record keystrokes made by users.

title: Kimchi Premium
description: Kimchi premium is a phenomenon occurring in South Korean crypto exchanges, making valuations appear higher than on other international exchanges.

title: Klinger Oscillator
description: The Klinger volume oscillator is a volume-based technical indicator that compares volume to price to forecast price reversals in the financial markets.

title: Know Your Customer (KYC)
description: Short for Know Your Customer, these are checks that crypto exchanges and trading platforms must complete to verify the identity of their customers. KYC is a set of principles for identifying and verifying the identity of banking clients to adhere to anti-money laundering regulations, but also to ensure that the bank has detailed information on the clients' risk tolerance, investment knowledge, and financial standing. KYC, or Know Your Customer, refers to the process by which a financial service provider must gather and verify information about their customers on registration. These requirements are enforced by governing bodies in both the customer and the business’s jurisdictions.

title: KYC
description: See Know Your Customer.

title: Lachesis
description: The consensus mechanism of Fantom blockchain.

title: Lambo
description: Slang for the type of car that many crypto enthusiasts aspire to buy when their digital assets 'moon' — or rise in value substantially.

title: Large Cap
description: Well-established projects and organizations having a market capitalization of $10 billion or above are called large cap or big cap projects/organizations.

title: LARP in Crypto
description: LARP (Live Action Role Playing) in crypto refers to a strategy where traders or influencers attempt to manipulate market sentiment through false narratives or manufactured hype.

title: Laser Eyes
description: Laser eyes is a viral Twitter meme that is used by Bitcoiners who attempt to push the price of BTC to its new all-time highs. It was originated with a hashtag, #LaserRayUntil100, back in February 2021.

title: Law of Accelerating Returns
description: The Law of Accelerating Returns is a hypothesis by Ray Kurzweil based on the observations that technologies (or any evolutionary system) tend to progress in an exponential fashion.

title: Layer 0
description: Layer 0 is a network framework running beneath the blockchain. It is made up of protocols, connections, hardware, miners, and everything else that forms the foundation of the blockchain ecosystem.

title: Layer-1 Blockchain
description: A layer-1 blockchain is a set of solutions that improve the base protocol itself.

title: Layer 2
description: Layer 2 is the name given to a scaling solution that enables high throughput of transactions whilst fully inheriting the security of the underlying blockchain that it is built on.

title: Leased Proof of Stake (LPoS)
description: Leased Proof of Stake (LPoS) is a consensus mechanism that allows cryptocurrency holders to lease their coins to nodes on a network.

title: Ledger
description: A ledger is a record-keeping system for recording and totaling economic transactions; in banking, the ledger was the principal book or 'bank book', containing all debit and credit account records of the bank. A ledger refers to a database of transactions. In the context of cryptocurrencies, the ledger is the transaction history of a given cryptocurrency as stored on the blockchain. A record of financial transactions that cannot be changed, only appended with new transactions.

title: Ledger Nano
description: Ledger Nano is a popular USB cold wallet to store cryptocurrencies.

title: Leverage
description: Money that a trader borrows from a brokerage, enabling them to gain far greater exposure to a position than what their capital allows.

title: Leveraged Tokens
description: In the world of cryptocurrencies, leveraged tokens give you a leveraged position in trading, meaning that your earnings and losses are multiplied.

title: libp2p
description: Libp2p is an open network protocol for decentralized peer-to-peer networking.

title: Light Node
description: Light nodes are typically downloaded wallets and are connected to full nodes to further validate the information that is stored on the blockchain. A Lightweight Node is a component of a network built on DLT that performs basic and limited functions contributing to authenticating and validating the transactions on the network.

title: Lightning Network
description: A second-layer protocol that is designed to solve Bitcoin’s scalability problem by allowing transactions to be processed more quickly. The Lightning Network is a payment protocol that was created on top of blockchain-based cryptocurrencies such as Bitcoin. It reduces the number of interactions on the blockchain and thus enables faster transactions and thus higher levels of scalability.

title: Limit Order
description: A limit order is a type of order to purchase or sell a security at a specified price or a better one. An order placed on an exchange to buy or sell an asset at a certain price or better. A buy order will be executed at a target price or higher, while a sell will only occur at a chosen or lower price.

title: Limit Order/Limit Buy/Limit Sell
description: Tools that enable traders to automatically buy or sell cryptocurrencies on a trading platform when a certain price target is reached.

title: Liquidity
description: Liquidity indicates how easy it is to convert a cryptocurrency into cash quickly — and whether this can be achieved without the asset’s value suffering. Liquidity can refer to two things. Most commonly, it is used to describe the quality of a cryptocurrency to be freely bought and sold. It can be used to mean the amount of cryptocurrencies available to trade within a liquidity pool on a decentralized exchange.

title: Liquidity Bootstrapping Pool (LBP)
description: A liquidity bootstrapping pool is essentially a contract that manages a core pool containing tokens to be used on an exchange.

title: Liquidity Hooks
description: A Liquidity Hook is a highly composable plugin for liquidity, enabling protocols to seamlessly integrate with platforms for capital access and developing yield/trading strategies.

title: Liquidity Mining
description: Liquidity mining is a mechanism or process in which participants supply cryptocurrencies into liquidity pools, and are rewarded with fees and tokens based on their share of the total pool liquidity.

title: Liquidity Pool
description: Liquidity pools are crypto assets that are kept to facilitate the trading of trading pairs on decentralized exchanges.

title: Liquidity Provider
description: Liquidity providers are decentralized exchange users who fund a liquidity pool with tokens they own.

title: Liquidity Provider Tokens (LP Tokens)
description: Liquidity provider tokens or LP tokens are tokens issued to liquidity providers on a decentralized exchange (DEX) that run on an automated market maker (AMM) protocol.

title: Liveness
description: A guarantee that a system will continue to provide data, and that no centralized authority can shut down its services.

title: LMD GHOST
description: LMD GHOST (aka the GHOST Protocol) is a fork-choice rule that allows the nodes in a blockchain network to reach an agreement on the valid state of the ledger.

title: Loan-to-value (LTV)
description: Used by lenders, loan-to-value (LTV) is the assessment ratio of risk involved while approving a loan. Simply, it is the ratio of the loan's value to the value of the collateral.

title: Location Swap
description: Location swap allows the change of claim to the assets manifested in the form of a token with no effect on other attributes.

title: Long
description: A situation where you buy a cryptocurrency with the expectation of selling it at a higher price for profit later.

title: Longing (Long Position)
description: A long position (longing) refers to the situation where an investor buys a cryptocurrency or any other financial instrument to sell it later when the price goes high.

title: Lovelace
description: The smallest denomination of ADA is called Lovelace.

title: Lower High
description: A lower high is when the price of a cryptocurrency closes at a high but lower than the previous day.

title: Lower Low
description: A lower low is when the price of a cryptocurrency closes lower than the previous day, which itself closed at a low.

title: Mainchain
description: A mainchain is the base blockchain layer where all transactions are processed and finalized.

title: Mainnet
description: An independent blockchain running its own network with its own technology and protocol.

title: Mainnet Swap
description: Mainnet swap refers to the shift of a cryptocurrency project from one blockchain network to another (which in most cases is its own native blockchain network).

title: Maker Protocol (MakerDAO)
description: The Maker Protocol allows users to leverage their assets as collateral that are approved by the Maker governance in order to get rewarded with DAI.

title: Malware
description: Malware or malicious software refers to harmful programs utilized by bad actors to illegally access and/or compromise a computer, network, or server.

title: Man-in-the-Middle Attack (MITM)
description: A man-in-the-middle attack (MITM) attack is a general term for a cyberattack where a perpetrator positions himself in a conversation between two parties either to secretly eavesdrop.

title: Margin Call
description: When an investor’s account value falls below the margin maintenance amount.

title: Margin Trading
description: A practice where a trader uses borrowed funds from a broker to trade a cryptocurrency. It is a method of trading that involves using borrowed funds. Compared to ordinary trading, it provides access to greater sums of money, allowing traders to leverage their positions and maximize their profits, while exposing them also to higher risks.

title: Market
description: An area or arena, online or offline, in which commercial dealings are conducted.

title: Market Balances
description: Market balances refer to the outstanding amount of tokens or coins after a trade has been made on a decentralized exchange (DEX).

title: Market Capitalization/Market Cap/MCAP
description: Total capitalization of a cryptocurrency’s price. It is one of the ways to rank the relative size of a cryptocurrency. Market capitalization traditionally refers to the total market value of a company's outstanding shares; in the context of cryptocurrencies, it denotes the total value of a project's tokens or coins in circulation. The price of an asset multiplied by circulating supply represents the total value of a given asset and its market. It is often used as a measurement of a project's success.

title: Market Maker, Market Taker
description: The maker places an order (to buy or sell at a quoted price), while a taker accepts that placed order (to execute the buy or sell at the quoted price).

title: Market Making as a Service (MMaaS)
description: Market Making as a Service (MMaaS) is a technology service that enables token issuers to set their strategies in market making, allowing them to trade and manage their own liquidity.

title: Market Order/Market Buy/Market Sell
description: A purchase or sale of a cryptocurrency on an exchange at the current best available price.

title: Market Signal
description: Through signaling, market participants are essentially creating a volatile market which can help to point out the opportunities to the investors.

title: Markets in Crypto-Assets (MiCA)
description: The Markets in Crypto-Assets (MiCA) regulation is a comprehensive regulatory framework instituted by the European Union (EU) to govern the issuance, trading, and provision of services related to crypto.

title: Marlowe
description: Developed by Input Output Hong Kong (IOHK), Marlowe is an easy-to-use programming language for experts with no programming expertise or knowledge, enabling them to write smart contracts for financial products.

title: Masternodes
description: Masternodes are a server maintained by its owner, somewhat like full nodes, but with additional functionalities such as anonymizing transactions, clearing transactions, and participating in governance and voting. It was initially popularized by Dash to reward owners of these servers for maintaining a service for the blockchain.

title: Max Supply
description: The best approximation of the maximum amount of coins that will ever exist in the lifetime of the cryptocurrency. The hard cap is the maximum number of coins that will ever be created (e.g., 21 million for Bitcoin); not all cryptocurrencies have a hard cap (e.g., Ether).

title: Maximal Extractable Value (MEV)
description: Maximal extractable value (MEV) is a measure of the profit a miner can make through their ability to arbitrarily include, exclude, or re-order transactions within the blocks they produce.

title: Medium of Exchange
description: A medium of exchange can be seen as an intermediary instrument or system that is used to facilitate a sale, purchase, or even trade of goods between parties.

title: Megahashes Per Second
description: Megahashes per second (MH/s) is a unit of measurement that equals one million hashes per second.

title: Meme Economy
description: Meme Economy is a satirical online subculture in which memes are addressed in financial language as if they were commodities or capital assets with varying prices.

title: Memecoin
description: Memecoins are the crypto tokens created as a joke or meme and claim to offer huge gains to holders.

title: Memorandum of Understanding (MoU)
description: A Memorandum of Understanding (MoU) is defined as a written agreement between two or more parties, which is legally-non binding.

title: Mempool
description: A mempool is the node's collection of all of the unconfirmed transactions that it has seen.

title: Mercenary Capital
description: Mercenary capital refers to the opportunistic capital provided by investors seeking to take advantage of the short-term incentive programs conducted by a platform for individual gain.

title: Merkle Tree
description: A tree structure in cryptography, in which every leaf node is labelled with the hash of a data block and every non-leaf node is labelled with the cryptographic hash of the labels of its child nodes. Hash trees allow efficient and secure verification of the contents of blockchains, as each change propagates upwards so verification can be done by simply looking at the top hash.

title: Meta Mask
description: An online digital wallet that allows users to manage, transfer, and receive Ethereum, operating as an extension to a regular browser. MetaMask is a browser extension for accessing dapps; as such, it can also serve as a digital wallet.

title: Metatransaction
description: A metatransaction executes a transaction signed by another party on behalf of the original signer, ferrying this original transaction onto the public blockchain rather than the person having to do it themselves.

title: Metaverse
description: A metaverse is a digital universe that contains all the aspects of the real world, such as real-time interactions and economies. It offers a unique experience to end-users.

title: Metaverse-as-a-Service (MaaS)
description: Metaverse-as-a-Service (MaaS) is a novel technological solution that anyone can use to build their own metaverses. The service provider offers the necessary infrastructure to enable both developers and non-technical users to create immersive virtual experiences alongside additional features like in-game tokenomics, NFTs, play-to-earn models, and much more.

title: Metcalfe’s Law
description: Metcalfe’s Law is a way of describing the use of networks and highlights that the more users a network has, the more valuable it is to each individual user.

title: MEV Protection
description: MEV protection refers to strategies and tools designed to shield crypto transactions from Maximal Extractable Value (MEV) exploitation.

title: Micro Cap
description: A digital asset with a very small market capitalization is referred to as a micro-cap stock/asset/cryptocurrency in the financial sector.

title: MicroBitcoin (uBTC)
description: One millionth of a bitcoin or 0.000001 of a bitcoin. Often confused as a fork of Bitcoin.

title: Microchain
description: Microchains are lightweight chains that operate in parallel within a common set of validators. In a microchain-based network, the role of proposing blocks is separate from the role of validating them.

title: Micropayment
description: A micropayment is essentially a small transaction that is carried out online and can be as small as a fraction of a cent.

title: Microtransaction
description: A business model where very small payments can be made in exchange for common digital goods and services, such as pages of an ebook or items in a game.

title: Mid Cap
description: Mid cap is a measure derived from a mathematical computation that determines the market value of a cryptocurrency with a market capitalization ranging from $1 billion to $10 billion.

title: MilliBitcoin
description: MilliBitcoin, also known as mBTC, is a sub-unit of Bitcoin which is equivalent to the one-thousandth of a BTC, the most famous and valuable cryptocurrency in the world.

title: Mimetic Theory
description: Mimetic theory explains human behavior and culture, and when understood contextually in economics, it deals with how things become desirable to individuals.

title: Mineable
description: Some cryptocurrencies have a system through which miners can be rewarded with newly-created cryptocurrencies for creating blocks through contributing their hash power. Cryptocurrencies with this ability to generate new cryptocurrencies through the process of confirmation are said to be mineable.

title: Miners
description: Contributors to a blockchain taking part in the process of mining. They can be professional miners or organizations with large-scale operations, or hobbyists who set up mining rigs at home or in the office. Mining is the process of contributing computing resources to a blockchain network in order to create new blocks. In exchange, miners earn rewards.

title: Minimum Collateralization Ratio (MCR)
description: Minimum Collateralization Ratio (MCR) is the least amount of collateral that needs to be pledged against a given loan.

title: Minimum Viable Product (MVP)
description: A minimum viable product (MVP) is a product that has enough features to attract early-adopter customers and validate a product idea. A minimum viable product is an early version of a product with just sufficient features to satisfy early adopters and to collect feedback for future product development.

title: Mining
description: Cryptocurrency mining is a process where blocks are added to a blockchain, verifying transactions. It is also the process through which new Bitcoin and some altcoins are created. Mining describes the process of creating new blocks on a DLT system such as the blockchain by validating transactions and demonstrating PoW and as a result being rewarded with newly created coins or tokens. Mining is the process of contributing computing resources to a blockchain network in order to create new blocks. In exchange, miners earn rewards.

title: Mining Algorithm
description: An integral component of blockchain, a mining algorithm is the set of rules or instructions that a computer follows to generate a valid block.

title: Mining as a Service (MaaS)
description: Cloud mining or mining-as-a-service allows users to rent the mining capacity of hardware in companies.

title: Mining Contract
description: Another term for cloud mining, where users can rent or invest in mining capacity online.

title: Mining Difficulty
description: The mining difficulty of a cryptocurrency is how difficult it is to find the right hash for the next block.

title: Mining Farm
description: A mining farm is when a group of miners mine together for a variety of advantageous reasons, like energy use.

title: Mining Pool
description: An arrangement where a number of miners pool their resources to increase their chances of finding the next block. A mining pool is a group or community of miners that have pooled their computing or hashing power together. Since miners in proof-of-work blockchains all compete to be the first to create new valid blocks, pooling can help these participants increase or level out their mining profits.

title: Mining Reward
description: The income that miners receive after finding and validating a block. The block reward is the compensation that a miner can claim for creating a block on the blockchain.

title: Mining Rewards
description: Mining rewards are the rewards that crypto miners receive for mining a new block on the blockchain.

title: Mining Rig
description: Equipment that is used for mining cryptocurrencies. A mining rig is a computer system especially designed for carrying out mining tasks and is thus typically using ASIC technology.

title: Minnow
description: An alternative term to Fish, describing someone with a small crypto investment.

title: Minting
description: Minting is the process of generating new coins using the proof-of-stake mechanism and adding them to the circulation to be traded.

title: Mnemonic Phrase
description: A list of words used in sequence to access or restore your crypto assets.

title: Mnemonics
description: Mnemonics are memory aids with a system such as letters or associations that help in recall. See Mnemonic Phrase.

title: Mobile Wallet
description: A mobile wallet is a crypto wallet installed on a mobile device. See digital wallet.

title: Modular Proof Verification
description: Modular proof verification is a process where confirming the validity and correctness of a ZK Proof, one of the most expensive parts of running ZK rollups and ZK apps, is offloaded to a dedicated service.

title: Moloch DAO
description: Moloch DAO can refer to the DAO framework, a description of a DAO that uses the framework, or the name of the Ethereum grant-giving DAO that originally created the framework.

title: Monetary Authority of Singapore (MAS)
description: The Monetary Authority of Singapore is the central bank managing money supply and regulating interest rates, inflation rate, and value for Singaporean currency.

title: Monetary Policy
description: Monetary policy can be defined as a set of rules that a country’s central bank makes to control the supply of money.

title: Money
description: Money is a widely used medium of exchange. It facilitates trade and gives people a way to store wealth.

title: Money Flow Index (MFI)
description: The Money Flow Index (MFI) is a technical indicator that measures the buying or selling pressure of an asset through price and volume.

title: Money Laundering
description: Money laundering is a technique used for illegal businesses to hide their money from the authorities.

title: Money Market
description: A financial market where short-term lending or borrowing takes place.

title: Money Transfer License
description: Money transmitter businesses need to acquire MTL or Money Transmitter License to be able to operate legally.

title: Money Transmitter
description: A business entity that provides money transfer services or payment instruments.

title: Monopoly
description: Monopoly is a type of market structure in which there is only one seller of a particular good or service.

title: Moon
description: A situation where there is a continuous upward movement in the price of a cryptocurrency. Often used in communities to question when a cryptocurrency will experience such a phenomenon, saying 'When moon?' It is usually combined with 'When Lambo?'

title: Moore's Law
description: Moore's Law states that computers' speed and capability will increase every year, even as cost goes down.

title: Motoko Programming Language (DFINITY)
description: The programming language for developing projects to run on the Internet Computer blockchain.

title: Move (Programming Language)
description: Move is a programming language initially used to build the Diem blockchain. It was created by the Diem Association, which was a tech consortium backed by Meta.

title: Move-to-Earn
