description: One of the newest blockchain-powered innovations, move-to-earn is a concept that encourages users to stay physically active by incentivizing them with crypto-based rewards.

title: Moving Average (MA)
description: Moving Average (MA) is a technical indicator that reacts to the trends of the financial markets and is used by market experts to predict the direction of an asset's trend.

title: Moving Average Convergence Divergence (MACD)
description: A technical analysis method.

title: Mt. Gox
description: A crypto exchange for buying and selling Bitcoin that closed in 2014 after a major hack.

title: Multi-Chain
description: Multi-chain is a foundational term that promotes blockchain interoperability and decentralization.

title: Multi-Coin Wallet
description: A multi-coin wallet, also known as a multi-chain wallet, allows users to keep more than one crypto asset belonging to multiple blockchain networks.

title: Multi-Level Marketing
description: Multi-level marketing is a business model that involves selling products on behalf of a company by unsalaried and hierarchical sales teams – earning commissions based on their sales and recruiting efforts.

title: Multi-Party Computation
description: Multi-party computation (MPC) is a cryptographic mechanism that distributes computation operations across several parties where no singular entity can see the other parties’ data.

title: Multi-Party Computation as-a-Service
description: Multi-party computation (MPC) as-a-service is a business model where consumers (enterprises and individuals) can rent MPC nodes from an MPC service provider instead of outright buying them or building them from scratch.

title: Multi-Signature (Multi-Sig)
description: An added layer of security by requiring more than one key to authorize a transaction.

title: Multiple Data Availability (MultiDA)
description: This blockchain architecture uses more than one data availability (DA) service to ensure data redundancy.

title: Multisignature
description: Multisignature crypto wallets require more than a single signature to sign a transaction.

title: Mutual Credit Line
description: Mutual credit is a multilateral exchange network in which money created endogenously within the network serves as a medium of exchange.

title: My Story (VeChain)
description: A digital assurance system built on a blockchain by DNV and VeChain.

title: Name Wrapper
description: Name Wrapper is a smart contract that allows all registered ENS names to be converted into NFTs, expanding their customizability.

title: Negative Volume Index (NVI)
description: The negative volume index (NVI) is a powerful technical indicator that shows how price is being influenced by low-volume timeframes.

title: Network
description: A network refers to all nodes in the operation of a blockchain at any given moment in time.

title: Network Fees
description: A network fee is required to ensure your transaction is processed on the Bitcoin or Ethereum network. The fee is used as an incentive to reward network participants, like miners and validators, for processing transactions and helping to secure the network.

title: Network Latency
description: Network latency refers to the amount of time it takes for a computer on one network to communicate with a computer on another network.

title: Network States
description: A network state is a community that establishes a degree of post-Westphalian autonomy or sovereignty, which is then used to develop a self-governing society with its own culture, economy, rules, legal frameworks, and formal institutions.

title: Network-Enhanced Virtual Machine (NEVM)
description: A Network-Enhanced Virtual Machine (NEVM) combines the best attributes of the Bitcoin (BTC) and Ethereum (ETH) networks, enabling smart contracts to leverage unprecedented levels of interoperability and scalability to support broad mass adoption.

title: Newb
description: A newb is someone that is new to a certain industry.

title: NFT Royalties
description: NFT royalties are a way for creators to earn a percentage of the sale value each time their NFT is sold on the secondary market.

title: NFT (Non-Fungible Token)
description: A non-fungible token is a type of Ethereum token. Like other tokens, they can be transferred between wallets and used in smart contracts, but they are not 'fungible'—meaning they cannot be replicated or subdivided and each unit is unique. These have seen significant traction over the last year as big names in the entertainment and art worlds have released their own artworks and digital collectibles, with some selling for millions of dollars on platforms like OpenSea. Non-fungible tokens (NFTs) are cryptocurrencies that do not possess the property of fungibility. An NFT is a non-fungible token, i.e., a token that bears some unique information; NFTs are therefore oftentimes considered collectable tokens.

title: NGMI
description: NGMI stands for 'Not Gonna Make It,' and is a slang that's become increasingly popular in the cryptocurrency community. At its core, NGMI is a dismissive and pessimistic expression used to convey doubt, skepticism, or a lack of confidence in something – whether it's a particular investment, a crypto project, or even someone's trading strategy.

title: Nick Szabo
description: Nick Szabo is the inventor of Bit Gold and the use of smart contracts.

title: Nifty Gateway
description: Nifty Gateway is an NFT platform owned by the Winklevoss twins.

title: No-Coiner
description: A no-coiner is someone who has no cryptocurrency in his or her investment portfolio and firmly believes that cryptocurrency in general will fail.

title: Node
description: The most basic unit of blockchain infrastructure that stores data. A node is a device on a network that represents a piece of a DLT and carries out certain tasks such as maintaining a copy of a ledger or parts thereof, processing transactions, etc. A node is a type of participant in the blockchain network that stores data and contributes to the consensus process to ensure that all new transactions and blocks are valid. Miners act as full nodes, but anyone may also operate a node to monitor the network without contributing computing resources.

title: Node.js
description: Node.js is a cross-platform JavaScript runtime environment that can be used for both servers and desktop apps.

title: Nominators
description: Nominators are one of two main actors who are involved in a blockchain network that uses the nominated proof-of-stake (NPoS) consensus algorithm.

title: Non-Custodial
description: Usually referring to the storage of keys, in relation to wallets or exchanges, a non-custodial setup is one in which private keys are held by the user directly.

title: Non-Fungible Assets
description: The term 'non-fungible assets' describes non-fungibility within a collection of similar assets being issued by a single party.

title: Nonce
description: When a transaction is hashed by a miner, an arbitrary number meant to be used only once is generated, called a nonce. A nonce is an arbitrary number or string that can be used only once in a cryptographic communication. A nonce is a random whole number that miners in proof-of-work blockchains like Bitcoin iterate over to solve the cryptographic puzzle that allows them to add a new block and earn the block reward. A miner’s goal is to find a nonce where the SHA-256 hashed output begins with a certain number of zeroes. This allows PoW networks to adjust the mining difficulty, requiring more zeros if blocks are being mined too fast.

title: Nonce Error
description: A nonce error occurs when a nonce - a number used only once - is misused or mishandled in a system.

title: Notarization on Blockchain
description: Notarization on blockchain simply uses the intrinsic nature and advantages of blockchain technology to let anyone create a timestamped artifact. The authorship and identity of this artifact can easily and securely be identified at any point.

title: Odysee
description: Odysee is a server-less video hosting and distribution platform built on top of the LBRY protocol.

title: Off-Chain
description: A transaction that is processed outside the blockchain network with an increased speed and reduced cost. Off chain is a property of a transaction denoting that it does not occur on a distributed ledger such as the blockchain. An off-chain transaction does not have to be validated; it is typically faster than an on-chain transaction.

title: Off-Chain Governance
description: Off-chain governance is a type of blockchain governance in which decisions are made informally, away from the primary code base of the blockchain.

title: Off-Chain Transaction
description: An off-chain transaction is defined as a second-layer protocol where the transactions occur on a network and move value outside of the blockchain.

title: Off-Ledger Currency
description: A currency that is created (minted) outside of the specified blockchain ledger but is accepted or used.

title: Office of the Comptroller of the Currency (OCC)
description: The Office of the Comptroller (OCC) is a U.S. Treasury branch that regulates all national banks, federal savings associations, federal branches, and foreign bank agencies.

title: Offline Storage
description: The act of storing cryptocurrencies in devices or systems not connected to the internet.

title: Offshore Account
description: An offshore account is defined as a foreign bank account with assets and investments set outside of your origin country or country of residence.

title: OHM Fork
description: OlympusDAO or Ohm Forks represent the upgrades to OlympusDAO’s codebase that has given birth to a variety of forked products.

title: Omnichain
description: Omnichain refers to a blockchain infrastructure that leverages chain abstraction to facilitate seamless interaction, data transfer, and transaction settlement across multiple blockchains.

title: On-Balance Volume (OBV)
description: On-balance volume (OBV) is a technical trading indicator that forecasts an asset's price movements based on the volume flow.

title: On-Chain
description: Transactions that are recorded on the blockchain itself and shared with all of the participants are done on-chain. On chain is a property of a transaction denoting that it occurs on a distributed ledger such as the blockchain, and thus that it is reflected there as soon as its state has been validated. An on-chain transaction is therefore regularly slower than an off-chain transaction.

title: On-Chain Governance
description: On-chain governance is a decentralized framework used for organizing and integrating updates/improvements to the blockchain networks.

title: On-Chain Structured Product
description: An on-chain structured product is a blockchain-based financial instrument that derives its value from tracking one or more underlying digital assets, such as Bitcoin, Ethereum, or stablecoins.

title: On-Ledger Currency
description: A currency that is both minted on the blockchain ledger and also used on the blockchain ledger, such as Bitcoin.

title: Onchain Fiat
description: Onchain fiat is a first-of-its-kind, fully authorized and regulated subset of stablecoins that allows for seamless transitions between traditional bank accounts and web3.

title: One Cancels the Other Order (OCO)
description: A situation where two orders for cryptocurrency are placed simultaneously, with a rule in place to enforce that if one is accepted, the other is cancelled.

title: Online Storage
description: The act of storing cryptocurrencies in devices or systems connected to the internet.

title: Ontorand Consensus Engine (Ontology)
description: Ontorand Consensus Engine is the VBFT consensus mechanism on the Ontology blockchain.

title: Open Finance (OpenFi)
description: OpenFi, short for 'Open Finance,' is a financial framework that integrates traditional finance (TradFi) with decentralized finance (DeFi).

title: Open Interest
description: Open interest refers to the total number of outstanding derivative contracts, specifically futures and options, that are held by market participants at the end of each trading session.

title: Open Source
description: Open source is a philosophy, with participants believing in the free and open sharing of information in pursuit of the greater common good. Open source is a principle according to which the source code of software is made available to anyone and for any purpose, such as inspection, modifying, and distribution by the copyright holder.

title: Open/Close
description: The price at which a cryptocurrency opens at a time period or the programming principle of software parts being extendable.

title: OpenSea
description: OpenSea is a decentralized P2P platform for NFTs.

title: Opera Mainnet (Fantom)
description: Opera (Fantom) is a permissionless, open-source framework that allows everyone to engage in the network through staking and governance.

title: Operating System (OS)
description: An operating system (OS) is a software and a resource manager that sits between the hardware and the user.

title: Optimistic Oracle
description: An optimistic oracle uses a dispute/arbitration process to ensure data accuracy. This compares to a price-feed oracle, which relies on nodes to provide consistent price feed data on-chain.

title: Optimistic Rollup
description: An optimistic rollup is a type of layer-2 scaling solution that relies on off-chain computation to record transactions in layer 2 trustlessly.

title: Option
description: A contract giving the buyer the right, but not the obligation, to buy or sell an underlying asset or instrument at a specified strike price.

title: Options Market
description: A public market for options, giving the buyer an option to buy or sell a cryptocurrency at a specific strike price, on or before a specific date.

title: Oracle Extractable Value (OEV)
description: Oracle Extractable Value (OEV) is a type of Maximal Extractable Value (MEV) that oracles can control.

title: Oracle Manipulation
description: Oracle manipulation is when an oracle smart contract is manipulated by hackers.

title: Oracles
description: An agent that finds and verifies information, bridging the real world and the blockchain by providing data to smart contracts for execution of said contracts under specified conditions.

title: Order Book
description: An order book comprises different key information regarding an asset.

title: Orphan
description: A valid block on the blockchain that is not part of the main chain.

title: Orphaned Block
description: An orphaned block is a block where the parent block does not exist or is unknown.

title: Ouroboros Praos
description: Ouroboros Praos is a proof-of-stake consensus mechanism developed by IOHK and is an updated version of Ouroboros Classic.

title: Over-Collateralization
description: Over-collateralization (OC) is the provision of collateral that is worth more than enough to cover potential losses in cases of default.

title: Over-the-Counter (OTC)
description: Over-the-counter is defined as a transaction made outside of an exchange, often peer-to-peer through private trades.

title: Over-the-Counter (OTC) Trading
description: Over-the-counter refers to the process of how securities are traded through a broker-dealer network as opposed to a centralized exchange.

title: Overbought
description: When a cryptocurrency has been purchased by more and more investors over time, with its price increasing for an extended period of time.

title: Oversold
description: When a cryptocurrency has been sold by more and more investors over time, with its price decreasing for an extended period of time.

title: P2P Bridge
description: A P2P bridge is a feature on decentralized exchanges (DEX) that enables two users to swap the same cryptocurrency across two blockchain protocols without involving a third party.

title: P2P DEX
description: P2P DEX, or peer-to-peer decentralized exchange, is a blockchain-based application that supports P2P trading.

title: P2P Trading
description: Peer-to-peer (P2P) trading involves decentralized transactions where two users swap cryptocurrencies directly with each other. Both buyers and sellers interact without the involvement of a third party.

title: Pair
description: Trade between one cryptocurrency and another, for example, the trading pair BTC/ETH.

title: Paper Trading
description: Paper trading or simulated trading is the practice of using a virtual transactional environment to simulate trading without the use of real capital.

title: Paper Wallet
description: A physical document containing your private key or seed phrase. A paper wallet is a type of cryptocurrency wallet that is stored on paper. The goal of these wallets is to reduce private key exposure to a minimum. This is a type of 'cold storage,' where private keys are not exposed to any internet-connected device that could be hacked or attacked. Today, paper wallets are largely seen as an unsafe option to store your crypto, since to generate such a wallet, they would have to use a central website and GUI—which are just as insecure and prone to attacks as any other centralized service.

title: Parachain
description: Parachains are application-specific data structures that run in parallel to each other within Polkadot. A parachain is a simpler form of blockchain, which builds on the security features provided by a relay chain rather than providing its own. One key feature of a parachain is that the computations it performs are inherently independent of the relay chain.

title: Participation Node
description: Participation nodes are present in the Algorand platform to help in conducting the Pure Proof of Stake (PPoS) consensus process.

title: Passive Income
description: Passive income is money produced from investments that do not require the earner to be actively involved.

title: Password Manager
description: A password manager is a tool or software that stores all sorts of passwords needed for online applications and services.

title: Paul Le Roux
description: Paul Le Roux is a criminal kingpin that many believe could be the founder of Bitcoin, Satoshi Nakamoto.

title: Payee
description: A payee is a party within an exchange of goods or even services that can receive payment.

title: Peer-to-Peer (P2P)
description: The decentralized interactions between parties in a distributed network, partitioning tasks or workloads between peers.

title: Peer-to-Peer (P2P) Lending
description: Crypto P2P lending refers to a practice of lending assets without the involvement of a middleman. Such loans rely on collateral material originally owned by borrowers.

title: Peg
description: A 'peg' is a specified price for the rate of exchange between two assets.

title: Pegged Currency
description: A stablecoin is a currency whose value is pegged to a real-world asset, such as a fiat currency.

title: Permissioned Ledger
description: A ledger designed with restrictions, such that only people or organizations requiring access have permission to access it.

title: Permissionless
description: Often used to describe blockchains, a system is said to be permissionless when there is no entity that can regulate who can use it and how it can be used.

title: Permissionless Market Creation
description: Permissionless market creation refers to a system in which anyone can set up a financial market that facilitates the exchange of funds or assets between two or more parties without needing approval.

title: Perpetual Contracts
description: A perpetual contract is a derivative similar to a futures contract but without an expiry date.

title: Perpetual Futures
description: Perpetual futures are like futures (derivative contracts or agreements to buy or sell a commodity at a specified price) but without an expiration date. They allow traders to leverage or hedge indefinitely, popular applications of this include Ethereum or Bitcoin.

title: Phishing
description: When a scammer pretends to be a trusted institution or person to trick people into revealing sensitive information such as Social Security numbers, passwords, banking details, etc., often through a malware link disguised as legitimate.

title: Phone Phishing
description: Phone phishing, also known as a telephone scam or vishing (voice phishing), refers to the practice of using fraudulent and malicious phone calls to extort money or sensitive information from victims.

title: Physical Bitcoins
description: A physical Bitcoin is a physical token that usually has an intricate design, as well as a public key and private key.

title: Platform
description: On CoinMarketCap, platform refers to the parent blockchain of tokens. It may also refer to a cryptocurrency exchange on which you may trade cryptocurrencies. See crypto protocol.

title: Play-to-Earn (Play2Earn)
description: The play-to-earn business model supports the notion of an open economy and gives financial rewards to players who bring value to its metaverse.

title: Player Payout
description: Player payouts is a new way of automatically paying online gaming participants immediately after the tournament ends.

title: Plutus (Cardano)
description: The scripting language used on Cardano blockchain for smart contract development.

title: Politeia (Decred)
description: A decentralized governance platform that allows Decred stakeholders to submit, track, and discuss proposals for suggestion and implementation of new ideas.

title: Ponzi Scheme
description: A fraudulent investment involving the payment of purported returns to existing investors from funds contributed by new investors.

title: Portfolio
description: A collection of cryptocurrencies or crypto assets held by an investment company, hedge fund, financial institution, or individual.

title: Portfolio Tracking
description: The act of tracking the movement and performance of assets of your financial holdings is termed portfolio tracking. This monitoring activity can cover stocks, commodities, mutual funds, ETFs, cryptocurrencies, and NFTs.

title: Position Size
description: Position size is a vital part of a trading strategy, since it is directly related to one’s potential profit or loss. Sizing a position also plays an important role in risk management as knowing when to execute larger or smaller trades, and when to increase or reduce the size of a position impacts profitability.

title: Post-Mine
description: The retroactive creation of new coins following a cryptocurrency’s launch, before public mining is possible.

title: Pre-IDO
description: Pre-IDO refers to token offerings before the actual initial DEX offering (IDO) takes place.

title: Pre-Mine
description: When some or all of a coin’s initial supply is generated during or before the public launch. Pre-mined is an attribute describing the process throughout which coins or tokens are being put in circulation: if pre-mined, all tokens will be put in circulation upon ICO.

title: Pre-Sale
description: The sale of a cryptocurrency, ahead of it going public, to specific investors. A pre-sale is a phase that can occur during an ICO and which is held in advance of the regular sale; select investors can then typically buy coins or tokens at a significant discount.

title: Prediction Market
description: Prediction Markets are exchange-traded markets where the future outcomes of events are traded. It indicates the confidence of the crowd in a specific future event.

title: Price Impact
description: The difference between market price and estimated price due to trade size.

title: Private Blockchain
description: A private blockchain is a type of blockchain in which only a single organization has authority over the network.

title: Private Key/Secret Key
description: A piece of code generated in asymmetric-key encryption process, paired with a public key, to be used in decrypting information hashed with the public key. A private key is a confidentially kept string of code that - when paired with a public key - sets off decryption algorithms, for instance permitting users to access their cryptocurrencies. A private key is used to identify the owner of a given cryptocurrency wallet. It acts much like a password, and anyone with a private key can access the funds from the associated wallet address.

title: Procedural Programming
description: Procedural programming refers to a series of instructions that inform a computer what it should do step-by-step to achieve the task.

title: Profit and Loss (P&L) Statement
description: A profit and loss (P&L) statement is a financial document that gives that sum up the earnings, costs, and expenditures incurred during a specified period.

title: Programmability
description: Programmability implies that something is capable of following instructions.

title: Programmable Privacy
description: Programmable privacy redefines data protection in decentralized applications (dApps), offering a flexible concept that empowers users and developers to personalize privacy settings.

title: Proof Market
description: A proof market is a decentralized marketplace where users can buy and sell cryptographic proofs to verify ownership, the validity of a particular transaction, and the authenticity of a piece of information or computation results.

title: Proof of Attendance Protocol
description: Proof of Attendance Protocol (POAP) refers to a business offering to use the ERC-721 NFT protocol on Ethereum to ascribe individuals a unique, non-fungible blockchain-based identifier that only the person can access.

title: Proof-of-Authority (PoA)
description: A blockchain consensus mechanism that delivers comparatively fast transactions using identity as a stake.

title: Proof-of-Burn (PoB)
description: Proof-of-burn is an attempt at creating a system that can prevent fraudulent transactions on a blockchain and can also improve the overall efficiency and functioning of the blockchain. A blockchain consensus mechanism aiming to bootstrap one blockchain to another with increased energy efficiency, by verifying that a cost was incurred in 'burning' a coin by sending it to an unspendable address.

title: Proof-of-Developer (PoD)
description: Any verification that provides evidence of a real, living software developer who created a cryptocurrency, in order to prevent an anonymous developer from making away with any raised funds without delivering a working model.

title: Proof-of-Donation
description: Proof-of-donation refers to the integration of charitable donations into the functionality of a blockchain.

title: Proof-of-History (PoH)
description: Proof of History (PoH) nodes have internal clocks that validate events and time. The incoming events are hashed using a verifiable delay function, also known as VDF, that increases the speed and scalability of a blockchain.

title: Proof-of-Immutability (PoIM)
description: Proof-of-Immutability (PoIM) is a novel blockchain architecture designed to protect data privacy in a distributed network. PoIM enables the blockchain to persist data in a decentralized and provably immutable manner, without having to distribute the data among nodes.

title: Proof of Personhood (PoP)
description: Proof of Personhood is a technological framework that verifies human identity in digital environments, distinguishing real humans from AI agents and bots through biometric validation, behavioral analysis, and optionally cryptographic verification.

title: Proof-of-Replication
description: Proof-of-replication (PoRep) is the way that a storage miner proves to the network that they are storing an entirely unique copy of a piece of data.

title: Proof of Reserves (PoR)
description: Proof of Reserves (PoR) is a method of using cryptographic verification to demonstrate possession of digital assets.

title: Proof-of-Spacetime
description: In simplest terms, PoSt means that someone can now guarantee that they are spending a certain amount of space for storage.

title: Proof-of-Stake (PoS)
description: A blockchain consensus mechanism in addition to Proof-of-Work that maintains the integrity of blockchain. Proof-of-stake, or PoS, is a consensus mechanism used by blockchains to ensure correct data is stored to the blockchain. In proof-of-stake blockchains, participants who deposit an amount of cryptocurrency to the network (known as 'staking') are given the opportunity to help generate new blocks and earn block rewards. Proof-of-stake is a consensus process that requires network participants to 'lock up', resp. 'stake', specific quantities of tokens used in the network for a short amount of time in order to 'vote' and generate network consensus; the participant can mine or validate block transactions corresponding to the quantity of coins or tokens he or she holds: the more coins or tokens are held by the miner, the more mining power he or she has.

title: Proof of Stake Authority (PoSA)
description: Proof of Stake Authority is an innovative consensus algorithm that represents a hybrid of Proof-of-Stake and Proof-of-Authority.

title: Proof-of-Time (PoT)
description: Proof-of-Time (PoT) is a decentralized, scalable, verifiably secure, and environmentally-friendly consensus algorithm that checks event data by choosing validators based on their ranking scores and a fixed stake.

title: Proof-of-Validation
description: Proof-of-validation (PoV) is a unique proof-of-stake (PoS) consensus mechanism that works to achieve consensus through staked validator nodes.

title: Proof-of-Work (PoW)
description: A blockchain consensus mechanism involving solving of computationally intensive puzzles to validate transactions and create new blocks. Proof-of-work, or PoW, is another consensus mechanism that leverages a network of computers called miners that verify transactions in exchange for crypto rewards. Anyone can become a miner, competing to create new blocks by using computing resources to solve a cryptographic puzzle called a hash. The first to do this earns the right to append their new block to the blockchain. Proof of work is a consensus process that requires a datum that is very costly to produce in terms of time and/or resources, yet which can be very simply verified by another party; PoW for Bitcoin is referred to as a nonce.

title: Proof of Work 2.0
description: Proof of Work 2.0 (PoW 2.0) is an evolution of the traditional Proof-of-Work consensus mechanism, integrating advancements in cryptography – primarily Zero-Knowledge (ZK) proofs – to achieve massive scalability while retaining the foundational security and decentralization benefits inherent to PoW.

title: Protocol
description: The set of rules that define interactions on a network, usually involving consensus, transaction validation, and network participation on a blockchain. See crypto protocol.

title: Protocol Layer
description: A protocol layer of the blockchain is defined as the rules and processes that govern how the network will operate. This is where we can find the different algorithms that determine how consensus is achieved and who gets to create new blocks.

title: Pseudonymous
description: Writing under a false name, such as 'Satoshi Nakamoto.'

title: Public Address
description: A public address is the cryptographic hash of a public key, allowing the user to use it as an address to request for payment. A public address is a hashed version of the public key. Generated alongside your private key as a pair, your public key is a long string of characters that is run through an algorithm to produce your wallet address.

title: Public Blockchain
description: A blockchain that can be accessed by anyone.

title: Public Key
description: A public key refers to a series of alphanumeric characters used to encrypt plain text messages into ciphertext. A public key is a publicly known string of code that is related to a private key and used in algorithms to encrypt data, for instance to send cryptocurrencies to a user’s address.

title: Public-Key Cryptography
description: Public-key cryptography is a collection of algorithms-based cryptographic procedures that are used to jumble secret data and make it look randomized.

title: Public-Key Infrastructure
description: A public key infrastructure (PKI) is a collection of roles, rules, hardware, software, and processes for creating, managing, distributing, using, storing, and revoking digital certificates, as well as managing public-key encryption.

title: Public Sale
description: A public sale is the final stage of an ICO in which a company offers its token to the public at a significant discount before the token is listed on cryptocurrency exchanges.

title: Pump and Dump (P&D) Scheme
description: A form of fraud involving the artificial inflation of the price of a cryptocurrency with false and misleading positive statements. Pump and dump is a type of scam in which the fraudster buys a coin or token and then artificially inflates its price using false and misleading positive statements in order to sell it thereafter at a higher price.

title: Pure Proof of Stake (PPoS)
description: Pure Proof of Stake (PPoS) is Algorand’s consensus mechanism that allows random selection of validators based on the consistency of their stakes.

title: Put Option
description: A put option contract offers an owner the opportunity, but not the compulsion, to buy an underlying security at a certain price within a given time frame.

title: Pyramid Scheme
description: A pyramid scheme is a scam with a hierarchical top-down structure.

title: Q
description: See QR Code.

title: QR Code
description: A machine-readable label that shows information encoded into a graphical black-and-white pattern.

title: Quant Zone (FTX Exchange)
description: A tool to create and share trading strategies on the FTX exchange.

title: Quantum Bit (Qubit)
description: A unit of measurement for the number of bits in quantum information, and is also called a 'qubit.'

title: Quantum Computing
description: A computer that harnesses phenomena from quantum mechanics in order to perform much more efficient computations than older, classical computer technologies are capable of.

title: Quasar Smart Contract (OMG Foundation)
description: A smart contract by OMG Network to solve layer-2 blockchain problems.

title: Quorum (Governance)
description: A quorum is the minimum number of members of an assembly or group that must be present at any of its meetings to make the proceedings of that meeting valid.

title: Radio Frequency Identification (RFID)
description: Radio Frequency Identification (RFID) is a type of technology that uses radio waves to passively identify a tagged item or individual.

title: Rage-quit
description: Rage-quit is the process where a member of a DAO exits part or all of their stake, leaves with a proportional share of the assets in the DAO’s treasury, and quits their participation.

title: Raiden Network
description: An off-chain scaling solution aiming to enable near-instant, low-fee, and scalable payments on the Ethereum blockchain, similar to Bitcoin's proposed Lightning Network. The Raiden Network is a complementary extension to the Ethereum network intended to allow for near-instant, low-fee, and scalable transactions with any ERC20 compatible token.

title: Rank
description: The relative position of a cryptocurrency by market capitalization.

title: Ransomware
description: Ransomware is a type of malware used by hackers to steal or encrypt their victims’ files to extort them for a ransom in exchange for file decryption or restoration.

title: Real World Assets (RWAs)
description: Real World Assets are off-chain assets, which are tokenized and brought on-chain for use in DeFi. Tokenization involves converting an asset's value into a digital token for representation and transactions on the blockchain.

title: Rebalancing
description: Rebalancing is the process of realigning the weightage of a portfolio of assets, that involves buying or selling assets periodically to maintain a targeted level of asset allocation and risk.

title: Rebase
description: A token designed so that the circulating supply adjusts automatically according to price fluctuations.

title: Recovery Seed
description: A recovery seed is a cryptographically derived security code composed of a list of random words, typically ranging between 12 and 14.

title: Recursion
description: Recursion refers to when a function calls on itself directly or indirectly in a circular loop(s).

title: Redundancy
description: The term refers to something that is in excess of that required for normal operation.

title: Regenerative Economy
description: A regenerative economy is a circular economic system that benefits the environment and society as a whole.

title: Regenerative Finance (ReFi)
description: Regenerative Finance can be defined as a system that regenerates its resource capacity over time.

title: Regens
description: Regen, like degen, is a term used to describe a crypto user who jumps into ReFi communities or invests in tokens that use blockchain technology to advance positive environmental impact.

title: Regional/Local/Community Currencies
description: A local currency is one that is spent in a certain geographical location. A regional currency refers to a local currency utilized in a larger area, and a community currency is often used within a specific community as a means of exchange.

title: Regulated
description: Regulation is when something is controlled by a specific set of rules.

title: Regulatory Compliance
description: Regulatory compliance is a set of mandates that every company or industry is required to follow to track accountability at work.

title: Rehypothecation
description: Rehypothecation is the practice where banks, and even the brokers themselves, use assets that have been posted as collateral by their clients for their own purposes.

title: REKT
description: A shorthand slang for 'wrecked,' describing a bad loss in a trade.

title: Relative Strength Index (RSI)
description: A form of technical analysis that serves as a momentum oscillator, measuring the speed and change of price movements.

title: Relay Chain
description: The Relay Chain is the central chain that is used by the Polkadot network. The relay chain is a blockchain that lends security to attached parachains and ensures secure message-passing between them.

title: Relay Nodes
description: Relay nodes help block-producing nodes communicate by guaranteeing that the authenticity of the core nodes and the blockchain is preserved, even if one or more relays are hacked.

title: Renewable Energy
description: Renewable energy is derived from solar, wind, and other 'indefinite' resources or operations that are renewed on a regular basis.

title: Repair Miners
description: Repair miners are a proposed type of mining node within the Filecoin network.

title: Replay Attack
description: Replay attacks are network security attacks where the comms between a sender and receiver is intercepted.

title: Replicated Ledger
description: A copy of a distributed ledger in a network that is distributed to all participants in a cryptocurrency network.

title: Replicated Security (RS)
description: Replicated Security (RS) is a new technology that lets a Cosmos blockchain share its economic security with another using the Inter-Blockchain Communication protocol (IBC).

title: Resistance (Line/Level)
description: The highest price level of an asset during a specific period.

title: Restaking
description: Restaking is a mechanism that allows validators on proof-of-stake (PoS) blockchain networks to redeploy their staked cryptocurrency across other PoS-based chains.

title: Retargeting
description: A retargeting algorithm, also referred to as a difficulty adjustment algorithm, is used on proof-of-work blockchains, such as Bitcoin.

title: Retire Your Bloodline
description: 'Retire your bloodline' is a popular crypto phrase that represents hitting it big enough in profits that not just you, but your entire family line would theoretically never have to work again.

title: Revenue Participation Tokens
description: Revenue participation tokens are a two-token system that uses one participation token and one payout token.

title: Reverse ICO
description: In a reverse ICO, an already-established company raises funds by selling tokens to shift its structure to decentralization.

title: Reverse Indicator
description: A person whom you may use as an indicator of how not to place buy or sell orders because they are always wrong at predicting price movements of cryptocurrencies.

title: Ring CT (Confidential Transactions)
description: RingCT is how transaction amounts are hidden in Monero.

title: Ring Miners
description: Ring miners are network participants in the Loopring protocol who manage order rings and ensure trades are completed for all parties involved.

title: Ring Signature
description: A cryptographic digital signature that obfuscates the identities of two parties within a transaction.

title: Roadmap
description: A roadmap is a high-level visual summary that helps map out the vision as well as the direction of a specific product.

title: Roger Ver
description: Roger Ver, also known as Bitcoin Jesus, has been a long-term proponent of Bitcoin and Bitcoin Cash.

title: ROI
description: Short for 'Return on Investment,' the ratio between the net profit and cost of investing.

title: Rollups-as-a-Service (RaaS)
description: Rollups-as-a-Service (RaaS) allows builders to build and launch their own rollups quickly. RaaS providers deliver end-to-end solutions like customizations of the underlying tech stack, no-code management, and one-click custom integrations with core infrastructure.

title: Roth IRA
description: Roth IRAs are generally the best investment option when you think your taxes will be higher in retirement than they are now.

title: Rough Consensus
description: Rough consensus, in its bare essence, refers to a method of decision-making that doesn't necessarily require unanimity.

title: Ruby (Programming Language)
description: Ruby is a high-level programming language designed with a focus on simplicity and code readability.

title: Rug Pull
description: A rug pull is a type of scam where developers abandon a project and take their investors' money.

title: Rust
description: Rust is a multi-paradigm programming language, similar to C++.

title: Ryuk Ransomware
description: Ryuk ransomware is a ransomware attack first discovered in August 2018.

title: S&P 500 (Standard and Poor's 500)
description: The Standard and Poor's 500, also known as S&P 500, is a stock market index that represents a list of 500 public companies located in the U.S and their performance in the market.

title: Sats or Satoshis
description: Sats are the smallest possible unit of Bitcoin (BTC), at 0.00000001 BTC. Named after Bitcoin’s enigmatic creator Satoshi Nakamoto, one Sat is equivalent to one hundred millionth of a Bitcoin. The smallest unit of bitcoin with a value of 0.00000001 BTC.

title: Satoshi (SATS)
description: The smallest unit of bitcoin with a value of 0.00000001 BTC.

title: Satoshi Nakamoto
description: The individual or group of individuals that created Bitcoin. Satoshi Nakamoto is a pseudonym used by an individual or a group of individuals who developed Bitcoin, devised the Bitcoin white paper, and developed the first blockchain.

title: Scale-Free Money
description: Scale-Free Money enables users to define values or objectives and then create and tie in specific denominations of money with those values.

title: Scaling Problem
description: The scaling problem is the limitations of a blockchain's transaction throughout and ability to have fast and low cast transactions.

title: Scaling Solution
description: A scaling solution is a method of enable a system to expand. A set of solutions built on top of a public blockchain to extend its scalability and efficiency, especially for micro-transactions or actions. Examples include Plasma, TrueBit, Lightning Network, and more.

title: Scam
description: A scheme that is designed to dupe people out of cash or crypto. A scam is an intentional deception to deprive another person of his or her right and to secure an unlawful gain and thus a dishonest scheme, resp. fraud; in the world of cryptocurrencies, various types of scam exist, varying from deceptive ICOs where the company has little more to offer than a white paper, to unjustified fees and costs charged by unregulated crypto exchanges and malware using devices to mine Bitcoin without the user's consent.

title: Scamcoin
description: Coins that are created as 'get rich quick schemes' by their developers are referred to as a scamcoin.

title: Scammer
description: A scammer is someone that participates in a fraudulent scheme.

title: Scholarship/Scholar
description: A scholarship is a popular practice in the Axie Infinity universe where managers lend their free Axies to new players (scholars) and earn passively from the battle rewards.

title: Script
description: A computer script is a list of commands that are executed by a certain program or scripting language.

title: Scripting Programming Language
description: A scripting language is a programming language that does not require the compilation step. It uses a high-level construct to carry out one command at a time.

title: Scrypt
description: An alternative proof-of-work (PoW) algorithm to SHA-256, used in Bitcoin mining. Scrypt mining relies more heavily on memory than on pure CPU power, aiming to reduce the advantage that ASICs have and hence increasing network participation and energy efficiency.

title: SegWit
description: SegWit, short for segregated witness, was an update to the Bitcoin network in August 2017 that aimed to increase the number of transactions that are able to fit onto each block by removing signature data from transactions. Segregated Witness (SegWit) is a Bitcoin Improvement Proposal (BIP) that aimed to fix transaction malleability on Bitcoin.

title: Segregated Witness (SegWit)
description: A Bitcoin Improvement Proposal (BIP) that aimed to fix transaction malleability on Bitcoin. SegWit is the label used for a soft fork implemented on some blockchain protocols, such as Bitcoin, which changes the transaction format so that it transfers parts of the transaction data out of the main block, thus reducing the effective size of transaction and thus allowing more transactions to fit into a single block without increasing the block size.

title: Selfish Mining
description: A situation in which a miner mines a new block but does not broadcast this new block to the other miners.

title: Sell Wall
description: A situation where a large limit order has been placed to sell when a cryptocurrency reaches a certain value. A sell wall is an expression describing a situation when the amount/size of sell orders for a particular coin or token is significantly larger than the number of buy orders.

title: Semantic Web
description: The ambition of the Semantic Web is to enable computers to manipulate information on our behalf.

title: Series B Funding
description: A Series B funding round is the second round of funding a business.

title: Settlement
description: Settlement refers to the process in which a user executes limit or market orders on an order-book-based DEX.

title: Settlement Layer
description: A settlement layer is a layer that essentially provides an anchor for an entire ecosystem.

title: SHA-256
description: A cryptographic hash function that generates a 256-bit signature for a text, used in Bitcoin proof-of-work (PoW). SHA-256 is a one-way cryptographic hashing algorithm that takes an input of any size and converts it to a random string of a fixed length. Many blockchain projects today make use of SHA-256 to enable proof-of-work mining for verifying transactions.

title: Shamir’s Secret Sharing
description: Shamir’s Secret Sharing is a scheme to securely share highly sensitive information such as encryption keys by splitting the information into multiple parts called shares.

title: Shanghai Upgrade
description: The Shanghai Upgrade will allow users to unstake and withdraw their ETH from the network.

title: Shard
description: Essentially, a shard is a portion of a blockchain network that has been split into multiple shards, which has its own data. See sharding.

title: Shard Chain
description: In the world of cryptocurrencies, sharding can reduce the network congestion as well as increase transactions per second through the creation of new chains.

title: Sharding
description: Sharding is a scaling approach that enables splitting of blockchain states into partitions containing states and transaction history, so that each shard can be processed in parallel. Sharding is a specifying type of database partitioning: it divides large databases, such as the blockchain, into smaller, faster, more easily manageable parts called database shards or shards.

title: Shelley Phase
description: The second era of Cardano – Shelley Phase is named after Percy Shelley, an English Poet.

title: Shiba Inu Token (SHIB)
description: The Shiba Inu token (SHIB), is a decentralized memecoin based on the Ethereum network.

title: Shielded Address
description: A shielded address is generated for a shielded transaction, where a payment is made using the blockchain network by keeping the related information private.

title: Shielded Transaction
description: A shielded transaction is essentially a transaction that is between two shielded addresses.

title: Shilling
description: The act of enthusiastically promoting a cryptocurrency or ICO project. Shilling is the process of someone heavily advertising a cryptocurrency; it is the first part of pump and dump schemes.

title: Shitcoin
description: A coin with no obvious potential value or usage. A shitcoin is a token or coin which has no value or utility (any longer).

title: SHO (Strong Holder Offering)
description: A strong holder offering (SHO) is a fundraising mechanism where eligible investors are chosen based on their on-chain activities and other proprietary data sets.

title: Short
description: A trading technique in which a trader borrows an asset in order to sell it, with the expectation that the price will continue to decline. In the event that the price does decline, the short seller will then buy the asset at this lower price in order to return it to the lender of the asset, making the difference in profit.

title: Short Squeeze
description: A short squeeze is an unusual market condition that causes the price of a coin to rise quickly, encouraging traders (who are betting against the price of the token) to buy it to avoid losses.

title: Side Channel Attack
description: A side channel attack is a hacking method that exploits a computer’s inherent 'tells' that unintentionally convey information.

title: Sidechain
description: A sidechain is a separate blockchain, tied to its parent blockchain via a two-way peg. A sidechain is a separate blockchain that is attached to its parent blockchain (mainchain) using a two-way peg.

title: Signal
description: Signals are a call to action to either buy or sell an asset.

title: Silk Road
description: An online black market that existed on the dark web, now shut down by the FBI.

title: SIM-Swap
description: A type of scam that exploits two-factor authentication measures.

title: Simple Agreement for Future Token (SAFT)
description: A Simple Agreement for Future Token (SAFT) is a contractual agreement at the time of launch of a token creating ownership rights for token investors at a future date. It is treated by market participants as financial security and hence is regulated by the relevant government institutions. The SAFT is an agreement conveying rights in tokens prior to the development of the tokens’ functionality; it ensures the delivery of tokens to the investors once a functioning application has been developed for that token.

title: Simple Ledger Protocol (SLP)
description: Simple Ledger Protocol (SLP) is a token system that works on top of Bitcoin Cash. It allows users to create their own tokens to represent anything they can dream of.

title: Simplified Payment Verification (SPV)
description: A lightweight client to verify blockchain transactions.

title: Skynet
description: A platform built on Sia blockchain for decentralized content storage.

title: Slashing
description: Slashing is a form of penalty on Proof of Stake (PoS) networks implemented to ensure accountability.

title: Slippage
description: Slippage happens when traders have to settle for a different price than what they initially requested due to a price movement.

title: Slot (Cardano)
description: The smallest time period in the Cardano blockchain.

title: Smart Contract
description: A smart contract is a computer protocol intended to facilitate, verify, or enforce a contract on the blockchain without third parties. A smart contract is a program that is stored on, and operated by, a blockchain network. Smart contract platforms like Ethereum allow developers to create decentralized applications that can work with cryptocurrencies in order to provide transparent financial tools and services for end users. A smart contract is an online contractual agreement based on the Ethereum blockchain that runs exactly as programmed without any possibility of downtime, censorship, fraud, or third-party interference.

title: Smart Contract Audit
description: A smart contract audit is a security check done by cybersecurity professionals meant to ensure that the on-chain code behind a smart contract is devoid of bugs or security vulnerabilities.

title: Smart Home
description: A smart home is a technology that allows to manage and automate household systems, such as lights, doors, thermostats, security alarms, and other connected equipment from a distance.

title: Smart Money
description: Smart money refers to the funds invested by individuals or entities with extensive financial experience, knowledge, and a keen eye for lucrative opportunities.

title: Smart Token
description: Smart tokens are simply regular tokens that not only transmit value they contain but also all the information needed to execute a transaction simultaneously.

title: Smart Treasury (Balancer)
description: Smart treasury is a mechanism for automatic buyback of project tokens in the DeFi industry.

title: Snapshot
description: A snapshot in the blockchain world is the act of documenting the status of a blockchain at a specific block height.

title: Sniping in Crypto
description: Crypto sniping refers to a trading strategy where traders aim to capitalize on brief market inefficiencies or newly launched tokens by executing trades at high speed.

title: Social Engineering
description: Social engineering is the use of deception to manipulate people into performing an action or giving away their secret information.

title: Soft Cap
description: The minimum amount that an initial coin offering (ICO) wants to raise. In the context of an ICO or an ITO, a soft cap is the minimum amount of money a project intends to raise. If this minimum amount is reached, the ICO or ITO is considered a success; although normally the project is expected to return the funds if the soft cap is not reached, there have been cases of projects keeping the funds despite the ICO or ITO not being successful, which may be considered a scam.

title: Soft Fork (Blockchain)
description: A protocol upgrade where only previously valid transactions are made invalid, with most soft forks requiring miners to upgrade their mining software. A soft fork is a specific form of a fork which occurs when the developers of a blockchain decide that changes must be made to the code so that previously valid blocks are made invalid; contrary to a hard fork, a soft fork requires that only a majority of nodes upgrade to the new version of the code; as old nodes will continue to recognize the new blocks as valid, a soft fork is backward-compatible.

title: Soft Peg
description: In finance, a soft peg is a method of keeping the value of a currency within a certain range against a reserve currency by using an exchange rate regime.

title: Software Library
description: A software library is a collection of code that can be referenced while developing other executable programs.

title: Software Stack
description: Software stack refers to a set of software products or components that work together to provide a particular function.

title: Software Wallet
description: A software wallet, also known as a wallet app, is an application that allows users to HODL, send, and receive cryptocurrencies. A software wallet is a digital wallet existing as an application on a computer and typically employs hot storage technology.

title: Solana Virtual Machine (SVM)
description: The Solana Virtual Machine, or SVM, is the system powering Solana's ability to handle thousands of transactions per second.

title: Solidity
description: The programming language used by Ethereum for developing smart contracts. Solidity is an object-oriented programming language that smart contracts can be written in; it can be used on various DLT platforms. Solidity is a high-level programming that is used to write smart contracts. Created by Gavin Wood, co-founder of Ethereum, Solidity allows developers to create immutable, unstoppable programs that anyone can interact with.

title: Solver
description: A solver is defined as an entity that executes a specific task requested by a user, known as an 'intent.'

title: Source Code
description: A collection of lines or computer-executable commands written in a high-level language is called a source code.

title: SPAC
description: A special purpose acquisition company (SPAC) is formed by investors in order to publicly list an organization without going through the troubles that come with the traditional IPO process.

title: Spear Phishing
description: Spear phishing is a personalized, targeted cyber attack that uses your interest to trick you into clicking a malicious link in an email.

title: Speculative Investment
description: Speculative investment is an investment where the investor expects a high return with a significant risk of loss.

title: Spoon (Blockchain)
description: A hard spoon is a meta-protocol that exists on top of a blockchain.

title: Spot
description: A contract or transaction buying or selling a cryptocurrency for immediate settlement, or payment and delivery, of the cryptocurrency on the market.

title: Spot Market
description: A public market in which cryptocurrencies are traded for immediate settlement. It contrasts with a futures market, in which settlement is due at a later date.

title: Spot Trading
description: Spot trading involves the immediate exchange of a financial instrument at the current price.

title: Spyware
description: Spyware is a malware that records all of the activities on an electronic device.

title: Stablecoin
description: A cryptocurrency with extremely low volatility, sometimes used as a means of portfolio diversification. Examples include gold-backed cryptocurrency or fiat-pegged cryptocurrency. Stable coins are coins that correlate relatively little with overall cryptocurrency markets. Oftentimes stablecoins are pegged to single fiat monies or to exchange-traded commodities.

title: Stacking Sats
description: 'Stacking sats' refers to the practice of accumulating small amounts of Bitcoin, typically expressed in 'satoshis,' the smallest unit of a Bitcoin.

title: Stagflation
description: Stagflation is a period during which an economy sees simultaneous stagnant growth and rising inflation (in other words, growth and inflation move in opposite directions).

title: Staking
description: Staking is a form of participation in a proof-of-stake (PoS) system to put your tokens in to serve as a validator to the blockchain and receive rewards. Staking is part of the proof of stake consensus process which requires network participants to 'lock up' specific quantities of tokens used in the network for a short amount of time in order to 'vote' and generate network consensus; typically, staking is rewarded depending on the size of the stake provided.

title: Staking Pool
description: Staking Pools allows users to combine their resources in order to increase their chances of earning rewards. This mechanism offers more staking power to the network to verify and validate new blocks. A mining pool is a group or community of miners that have pooled their computing or hashing power together. Since miners in proof-of-work blockchains all compete to be the first to create new valid blocks, pooling can help these participants increase or level out their mining profits.

title: Stale Block
description: A block which was successfully mined but not included on the current longest blockchain, usually because another block at the same height was added to the chain first.

title: State Channel
description: A second-layer scaling solution that reduces the total on-chain transactions necessary, moving the transactions off-chain and letting participants sign to the main chain after multiple off-chain transactions. State channels are secure transactions among DLT users that occur off the blockchain and thus minimize the use of on-chain operations; they are used, for instance, in the Raiden Network or Lightning Network.

title: Stochastic Oscillator
description: A stochastic oscillator is a popular technical indicator used for identifying overbought and oversold stock/asset/cryptocurrency levels that rely on an asset's price history, as it tend to fluctuate within a specific price range.

title: Stock-to-Flow Ratio
description: The stock-to-flow ratio is a metric used to measure the scarcity of a commodity, particularly precious metals and cryptocurrencies.

title: Stop-Loss Order
description: A stop-loss order in trading allows investors to determine the lowest price at which they are willing to sell an asset and trigger an automatic sell order when and if this price is reached.

title: Storage (Decentralized)
description: Decentralized storage refers to the concept of storing files online by splitting them into encrypted fragments and delegating these fragments to multiple nodes on a distributed network, e.g., a blockchain.

title: Storage Miners
description: Storage miners are cryptocurrency miners who rely on offering sufficient storage space for nodes to reach consensus and validate transactions.

title: Storage Node
description: Storage nodes are the principal part of the Storj decentralized cloud storage network.

title: Store of Value
description: A store of value is an asset, commodity, or currency that can be saved, retrieved as well as exchanged in the future without it losing any value.

title: Stroop
description: A stroop is the smallest unit of Lumen (XLM).

title: Subgraph Manifest
description: A component of Subgraph that entails information pertaining to subgraph’s data sources, templates, and more.

title: Subnet
description: A subnet is a smaller network within a larger network. Think of all the computers, printers, and other devices that make up a network as pieces of a huge jigsaw puzzle. A subnet is like a chunk of that jigsaw; it collects nodes that all have something in common.

title: Substrate
description: Substrate is a web app development framework developed by Parity Technologies.

title: Succinct Proofs of Random Access (SPoRA)
description: Succinct Proofs of Random Access (SPoRA) is a consensus mechanism used to confirm transactions and validate blocks on the Arweave decentralized network.

title: Supercomputer
description: A supercomputer is a superior version of a general-purpose computer, which has a significantly increased capacity and processing power.

title: Supercycle
description: In economics, a 'supercycle' describes an extended period characterized by the outsized growth of a particular asset or sector.

title: Supply and Demand
description: Supply and demand are the levels in an economy that determine the market's willingness to buy or sell assets or services, respectively.

title: Supply Chain
description: A supply chain is the collection of steps that a product or service needs to go through before reaching the final customer.

title: Supply Chain Attack
description: A supply chain attack is a tactic used by hackers to compromise third-party suppliers to major corporations, governments, and organizations to gain valuable information.

title: Support Level
description: A support level in crypto is when the price of a crypto asset stops depreciating because of increased supply from buyers that wish to buy at a certain price.

title: Surge (Ethereum)
description: The Ethereum Surge is a development stage of the Ethereum network. It includes a set of upgrades, most notably sharding.

title: Swarm
description: A swarm is a group of peers that are sharing the same torrent.

title: Swing Failure Pattern (SFP)
description: A swing failure pattern (SFP) is a trend reversal indicator that can be used to discover a weakness in the current trend and identify early reversal signs.

title: Swing Trading
description: Swing trading is a market trading technique that aims to profit from short to medium-term price changes in stocks, commodities, and/or currencies over a period of days or weeks.

title: Sybil Attack
description: Sybil attacks undermine an online network by creating many IDs, accounts, or nodes to upset the balance of power.

title: Symbol
description: The ticker of a cryptocurrency; for example, Bitcoin's symbol is BTC. An abbreviation used to uniquely identify cryptocurrencies.

title: Symmetric Key Cryptography
description: Symmetric key cryptography is a type of cryptography that uses a single key to both encrypt and decrypt data.

title: Synthetic Asset
description: Synthetic assets, sometimes referred to as synths, are a combination of cryptocurrencies and traditional derivative assets. In other words, synths are tokenized derivatives.

title: T-Address (Zcash)
description: T-addresses are one of the two types of addresses available for the privacy-focused cryptocurrency, Zcash, used when transparency is desired.

title: Taint
description: The percentage of cryptocurrency in an account that can be traced to another account.

title: Take Profit
description: A take-profit order is an act of selling cryptocurrency to secure profits. It is usually executed at a predetermined price when the trade is in profit.

title: Tamper-Proof Ledger
description: A tamper-proof ledger is essentially any system of records that has the fundamental properties of a blockchain distributed ledger.

title: Tangle
description: The Tangle is a blockchain alternative developed by IOTA, using directed acyclic graphs which only builds in one single direction and in a way that it never repeats, and is quantum-computing resistant. Tangle is a DLT that requires the user to approve of two previous transactions if he/she wants to carry out a new one; by doing so, the initiator of a new transaction indirectly confirms that a subsection of the Tangle is valid and that it conforms to the protocol rules; it thus represents Tangle’s proof of work; Tangle therefore merges the transaction-making process with the consensus mechanism.

title: Tap-to-Earn Crypto Games
description: Tap-to-earn crypto games are applications that allow users to earn crypto rewards by performing simple, repetitive actions - typically tapping or clicking on their device screens.

title: Taproot
description: Taproot is an instantiation of a soft fork for Bitcoin, intended to both improve privacy and improve other aspects tied to more complex transactions.

title: Tardigrade (Storj)
description: Tardigrade is the decentralized cloud storage service provided by the Storj platform.

title: TA or Technical Analysis
description: Technical analysis refers to the process of studying price charts and using various market indicators to determine the state of the market. This is a deep area of study for expert traders, who aim to use technical analysis to their advantage when trying to predict future market movements. Technical Analysis/Trend Analysis (TA) is an evaluation method involving statistical analyses of market activity, such as price and volume. Charts and other tools are used to identify patterns to underpin and drive investment decisions.

title: Technical Analysis/Trend Analysis (TA)
description: An evaluation method involving statistical analyses of market activity, such as price and volume. Charts and other tools are used to identify patterns to underpin and drive investment decisions.

title: Technical Indicators
description: A technical indicator is a statistical algorithm or pattern-based indication based on a security's or contract's historical price, volume, and/or open interest.

title: Tendermint
description: Tendermint is a consensus mechanism that allows applications to launch across different machines securely and consistently.

title: Terahashes Per Second
description: Terahashes per second (Th/s) is equivalent to 1 trillion (1,000,000,000,000) hashes per second, a unit that indicates the power of a computer or mining machine.

title: Testnet
description: An alternative blockchain used by developers for testing. Smart contract platforms like Ethereum and Binance Smart Chain provide developers a testing environment in which they can deploy and test smart contracts, called a testnet. These testing environments usually work the same as the main network, but where the cryptocurrencies used are valueless and can be acquired for free from a testnet faucet.

title: The Barbell Strategy
description: A method in which investors put their money in two extremes of high-risk and no-risk assets while ignoring the middle-risk assets.

title: The Cantillon Effect
description: Developed by Richard Cantillon, The Cantillon Effect is a change in relative prices resulting from a change in the money supply.

title: The DAO
description: The DAO is the first decentralized autonomous organization, which was created by a group of developers in April 2016.

title: The Merge (Ethereum 2.0)
description: The merge is a planned network upgrade that will combine the Ethereum mainnet and the Beacon Chain to transition from the proof-of-work consensus mechanism to the proof-of-stake system.

title: Think Long Term (TLT)
description: A mindset where you have a longer-term investment horizon of months to years.

title: This Is Gentlemen
description: Originally an error in writing the full 'This is it, gentlemen'. It is now used as an introduction for good news.

title: Throughput
description: Throughput is how many actions can be completed in a given time frame.

title: Ticker
description: An abbreviation used to uniquely identify cryptocurrencies.

title: Ticker Symbol
description: The ticker symbol is the unique combination of letters assigned to stocks or cryptocurrencies that makes them distinguishable on exchanges and other trading applications.

title: Time-Weighted Automated Market Maker (TWAMM)
description: Time-Weighted Automated Market Maker (TWAMM) aims to help traders execute large orders with minimal slippage and low gas fees without negatively affecting the price.

title: Time-weighted Average Price (TWAP)
description: The time-weighted average price (TWAP) is a trading indicator based on weighted average price, which shows the average price of an asset as it rises and falls during a given or specific time period.

title: Timelock/Locktime
description: A condition for a transaction to only be processed at a certain time or block on the blockchain.

title: Timestamp
description: A form of identification for when a certain transaction occurred. A timestamp is a set of information identifying the time at which an event is recorded by a computer; on the blockchain, timestamps show the chronological order of the blocks and mark the exact time of each transaction; timestamps prove what has happened when on the blockchain.

title: Tipset
description: A tipset is a set, rather than a chain, of blocks that make up a blockchain.

title: Token
description: Tokens are cryptocurrencies that are created and accounted for in DLT systems and represent an asset, a usage right, or a unit of value issued by an organization and are typically emitted throughout an ICO, ITOs, or private sale; oftentimes, tokens do not run on proprietary DLT systems, but use other cryptocurrencies' blockchains, most typically Ethereum. Tokens, distinct from cryptocurrencies, usually refer to tokens that have been issued by deploying a smart contract to the Ethereum blockchain. Thousands of tokens exist today, and many of the top 100 cryptocurrencies by market cap are in fact Ethereum tokens. A digital unit designed with utility in mind, providing access and use of a larger crypto economic system.

title: Token Economy
description: An economy of goods and services that can run without intermediaries and third parties with the help of the blockchain technology.

title: Token Generation Event (TGE)
description: The time at which a token is issued.

title: Token Issuance
description: Token issuance is the process of creating new tokens and adding them to the total token supply of a cryptocurrency.

title: Token Lockup
description: Token lockup refers to a time period during which cryptocurrency tokens cannot be exchanged or traded.

title: Token Migration
description: Token migration refers to the process of moving tokens from one blockchain to another as a result of a change in the blockchain.

title: Token Sale
description: A token sale refers to the initial offering of a cryptocurrency token to a private pool of investors before it officially goes on the market. Token sales is the process of tendering newly mined tokens or coins, oftentimes in the context of an ICO or STO.

title: Token Standard
description: The most common token standard today is the Ethereum Request for Comment or ERC.

title: Token Swap
description: Token swap refers to the direct exchange of a certain amount of one cryptocurrency token for another between users facilitated by a special exchange service. It can also be defined as the migration of a cryptocurrency token built on top of one blockchain platform to a different blockchain.

title: Tokenization
description: The process by which real-world assets are turned into something of digital value called a token, often subsequently able to offer ownership of parts of this asset to different owners. Tokenization is a method of protecting sensitive information by substituting a critical data element with a non-sensitive unique alphanumeric identifier, referred to as a token, that has no exploitable meaning or value to third parties. E.g., tokenization can be used to create a representation of a real-world asset by a digital token.

title: Tokenized Carbon Credits
description: Tokenized carbon credits represent carbon that has been avoided or removed from the environment. One metric tonne of carbon verifiably avoided or removed translates to one carbon credit.

title: Tokenized Identity
description: Tokenized identity refers to the representation of an identity as a digital token on a blockchain, enabling seamless, interoperable identity verification across platforms and applications.

title: Tokenized Securities
description: Tokenized securities are when the ownership of a security is materialized through the issuance of a token.

title: Tokenized Stocks
description: Tokenized Stocks are the digital assets that are traded on exchanges using blockchain technology.

title: Tokenomics
description: Tokenomics is the science of token economy which consists of a set of rules that governs a cryptocurrency's issuance and supply. Tokenomics (umbrella term that encompasses token and economics) is the economics underlying a token: it sets forth when, which quantity of tokens are issued and burned and for which purposes they can be used and when; it thus determines the framework for supply and demand.

title: TokenSets (Set Protocol)
description: TokenSets is a Set Protocol-based decentralized platform for crypto portfolio management.

title: Toll Bridge
description: A toll bridge is a bridge powered by a smart contract where a monetary value called a toll fee unlocks access to extra functionalities.

title: Tor
description: Tor is a decentralized network that anonymizes users' web traffic by encrypting it and routing it through a series of relays before it reaches its final destination.

title: Total Exchange Volume
description: Total exchange volume is a measure of the total value that has been traded on an exchange(s).

title: Total Supply
description: The total amount of coins in existence right now, minus any coins that have been verifiably burned. The total supply is the total amount of tokens or coins in existence at the present; it is equal to the total amount of coins/tokens mined minus any that have been verifiably burned.

title: Total Value Locked (TVL)
description: Total value locked represents the number of assets that are currently being staked in a specific protocol.

title: Trade Volume
description: The amount of the cryptocurrency that has been traded in the last 24 hours.

title: TradFi
description: TradFi, short for Traditional Finance, refers to the conventional financial system and institutions that have been the backbone of the global economy for centuries.

title: Trading Bot
description: A crypto trading bot is essentially a program that is designed to automate cryptocurrency asset trading on the behalf of the trader. A trading bot is an application that is directly linked to crypto exchanges oftentimes via an API and, programmed to trade cryptocurrencies following predefined algorithms.

title: Trading Tournament
description: Trading tournaments are unique crypto trading campaigns organized by cryptocurrency exchanges, encouraging users to trade more to win incentives, such as tokens, hardware wallets, and more.

title: Trading Volume
description: Trading volume refers to the total number of shares (or tokens/coins) that have been exchanged between buyers and sellers of a given asset during trading hours of a certain day.

title: TradingView
description: TradingView is an online web-based platform that provides investors and traders with a user-friendly interface to analyze financial markets using charts and technical indicators.

title: Transaction (TX)
description: The act of exchanging cryptocurrencies on a blockchain.

title: Transaction Fee
description: A payment for using the blockchain to transact. A per transaction fee is an expense a user must pay to an intermediary for conducting a transaction on a specific system. On cryptocurrency exchanges, fees are typically calculated based on a percentage of the notional order value for a matched trade; the fees typically differ between maker fee and taker fee. A network fee is required to ensure your transaction is processed on the Bitcoin or Ethereum network. The fee is used as an incentive to reward network participants, like miners and validators, for processing transactions and helping to secure the network.

title: Transaction ID (TXID)
description: A transaction ID (TXID) is the unique identification number of each blockchain transaction.

title: Transaction Triggers
description: Triggers can be set up on a blockchain, which group various transactions together to be executed when the designated conditions are met.

title: Transactions Per Second (TPS)
